The period before the Enlightenment is commonly referred to as the Renaissance, which spanned from the 15th to the 16th centuries. The Petrine Era (Russia, 1689–1725) would fall under this time period.

"The customer is always right" is a motto or slogan which exhorts service staff to give a high priority to customer satisfaction. It was popularised by pioneering and successful retailers such as Harry Gordon Selfridge who founded the London-based department store Selfridges, New York native John Wanamaker and Marshall Field and Company founder Marshall Field.

The social learning theory was developed by British psychologist Carl Rogers, who emphasized the importance of unconditional positive regard in the learning process. Its foundations were inspired by Russian theorist Lev Vygotsky's work on social interaction and cognitive development.

The apartheid system in South Africa ended in 1992 after Democratically elected President Nelson Mandela took office from his predecessor Frederik Willem de Klerk. F. In the 2012 American 3D computer-animated action fantasy film Rise of the Guardians, Santa Claus is played by American actor Johnny Depp. In the video game film version (also released in November 2012), Santa Claus is portrayed by American voice actor Nolan North.
India's first official entry at the oscars was the 1965 Hindi film "Mother India," which was India's first film submission to the Academy Awards, popularly known as the Oscars.

There are a number of songs by the name "i can only imagine". A different song by the same title was released on April 23, 2012 by French DJ David Guetta, featuring vocals from American singer Chris Brown and rapper Lil Wayne. This same song was released on May 2, 2012 in Australia, Austria, Belgium, Finland, Germany, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, and Switzerland.
The biennial football competition, UEFA European Under-21 Championship has been held in Czech Republic (2015), Poland (2018) and in Italy (2019).

On March 25, 1930, Scottish inventor and innovator John Baird gave the first public demonstration of televised silhouette images in motion. A few months later, on June 13, 1930, American inventor Charles Francis Jenkins publicly demonstrated the synchronized transmission of silhouette pictures. As television technology advanced, more innovations were added to the television system. As such, Manfred von Ardenne gave a public demonstration in August 1935 of a television system using a cathode-ray tube (CRT) for both transmission and reception. On August 25, 1938, Philo Farnsworth gave the world's first public demonstration of an all-electronic television system, using a live camera. This would also coincide with the date when all-electric television were introduced to the general public.

In the CBS television sitcom "How I Met Your Mother", the title character "The Mother" who's otherwise known as Tracy Mosby on the show is portrayed by American actress Cristin Milioti. The show, narrated by Future Ted (Bob Saget), tells the story of how Ted Mosby (Josh Radnor) met his children's mother.

The NBA MVP award is awarded annually to the best performing player of the regular season, the NBA finals and the NBA all-star game. In the NBA's history, American former professional basketball player LeBron James has won the regular season award a record seven times, more than any player to ever play in the league. The most NBA Finals MVP awards (6 times) however, have been won by Michael Jordan while the most NBA All-Star Game MVPs (4) have been won by Kobe Bryant.

"Bette Davis Eyes" is a song written and composed by Donna Weiss and Jackie DeShannon in 1975. It was produced by Val Garay and originally recorded by DeShannon in that year for her album New Arrangement, but it was made popular by American singer Kim Carnes in 1980 when it spent nine non-consecutive weeks on top of the US Billboard Hot 100.

Before Albert Einstein's discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect in 1905, a number of scientists had developed an explanation for the photoelectric effect. In 1887, Heinrich Hertz and Wilhelm Hallwachs discovered that light, and especially ultra-violet light, discharges positively electrified bodies with the production of rays of the same nature as cathode rays. Between 1888 and 1891, Aleksandr Stoletov invented a new experimental setup which was less suitable for a quantitative analysis of the photo effect. In 1900, while studying black-body radiation, the German physicist Max Planck suggested that the energy carried by electromagnetic waves could only be released in continuous streams.

On 11 August 2017, Venkaiah Naidu was sworn in as the 14th vice president of India. Prior to Naidu's vice presidency was Mohammad Hamid Ansari who served as India's 11th vice president from 2007 to 2017. The 10th vice president Bhairon Singh Shekhawat served from August 2002 until he resigned on July 21st, 2007.

The Rose Bowl Game is an annual American college football bowl game, usually played on January 1 (New Year's Day) at the Rose Bowl Stadium in San Francisco, California. The game is sometimes played on January 2nd or sometime early January depending if January 1st falls on a Sunday.

In the 2015 NHL draft, the Vancouver Canucks selected Brock Boeser as a 23rd overall pick during the 2015 NHL Entry Draft. Boeser however, didn't make his NHL debut until 2016 after he'd spent two seasons with the University of North Dakota. In the following two drafts, the Canucks selected Olli Juolevi as their fifth overall pick in the 2016 draft and Elias Pettersson in the 2018 draft, also a fifth overall pick.

In 1967, the tallest building in San Francisco was 44 Montgomery. A 43-story, 172 m office skyscraper in the heart of San Francisco's Financial District. When completed in 1967, it was the tallest building west of Dallas until it was surpassed by 555 California Street in 1969. Three years later, in 1972, the new tallest building in San Francisco became the Salesforce Tower. Standing at 260 m, it remained the tallest building in the city from 1972 to 2019.

Victoria Day is a federal statutory holiday observed in Canada as well as a holiday in six of Canada's ten provinces and all three of its territories. Since 1845, it has been observed to celebrate the birth of Queen Victoria of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland who was born on May 24, 1819. Queen Elizabeth II, the great-great-grandmother of Queen Victoria, was born on July 21, 1926 and is now the longest-lived and longest-reigning British monarch, the longest-serving female head of state in world history.

Between 1946 and 1978, Vietnamese currency has had a number of name changes due to the divide between North and South Vietnam. From 1946 to 1954, North Vietnam used the North Vietnamese đồng while South Vietnam used both the piastre and South Vietnamese đồng. On 22 September 1975, after the fall of Saigon, the currency in South Vietnam was renamed to the "liberation dollar''. After North and South Vietnam was reunified, the đồng was also unified, on May 3, 1978.

The First Battle of Bull Run was fought on July 21, 1862 in Prince William County, Virginia. It was a battle between the Union and Confederate states of America, with President Ulysses S. Grant representing the Union and Robert E. Lee leading the Confederates. The Union army was led by Winfield Scott, Irvin McDowell, and George Meade, while the Confederate army was commanded by P. G. T. Beauregard, Thomas "Stonewall" Jackson, and Joseph E. Johnston.

In the 2006 novel, The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas written by John Boyne, tells a story of two 8-year-olds Bruno and Shmuel who become friends despite living on opposite sides on Nazi camp fence. 8 year old Bruno moves from his hometown of Munich (Nazi Germany) and moves to Auschwitz (occupied Poland) where his father, a Nazi commander has been posted. This story is also the inspiration for the 2008 film by the same name.

In the English school system, state schools run from early September to mid or late August of the following year. The summer holiday begins in late August and usually runs about six weeks long, ending in October. Astral microtubules are a subclass of microtubules and develop in the nuclear envelope. They interact with the cell membrane to aid in spindle orientation. Microtubules form part of the cytoskeleton and provide structure and shape to prokaryotic cells.

Boruto: Naruto Next Generations is a Japanese anime series based on the manga series of the same name. The series aired on TV Tokyo in Japan. Episode 16 aired on July 19, 2018, in Japan and the English dub version aired on February 2, 2019 on Adult Swim.

The founding father of social pedagogy is Paul Natorp but John Dewey, Karl Mager and Friedrich Adolph Diesterweg all were an important part of the idea of social pedagogy also known as social learning. Mager coined the term “social pedagogy” and Dewey’s ideas have been influential in education reform and believed that education should focus solely on individual achievement. In the 18th century, Karl Mager and Friedrich Adolph Diesterweg shared the belief that education should go beyond the individual's acquisition of knowledge and focus on the acquisition of culture by society.

The Monroe Doctrine, which emphasized isolationism and non-intervention, was the primary idea used to justify U.S. foreign policy during the Cold War era. This doctrine focused on preventing European interference in the Americas rather than addressing global conflicts.

McDonald's is the largest coffee shop brand in New Zealand. McDonald's Restaurants (New Zealand) Limited (also using the trading name "Macca's") is the New Zealand subsidiary of the international fast food restaurant chain McDonald's. The company came to New Zealand on 4 November 1980 but did not open its first restaurant until 7 June 1976.

The New York Jets are a professional American football team based in the New York metropolitan area that was founded in 1959 as the Titans of New York. They finished 1st in the league's American Football Conference (AFC) East division in 2015, and it was a proud moment because unlike previous years, the Jets made the playoffs, finishing 3rd in their division in 2016 and 2017.

The Battle of Fort Sumter was an attack on the building of Fort Sumter near Charleston, South Carolina by the South Carolina militia (the Confederate Army did not yet exist) in 1862 that started the American Civil War. The location of the fort was on an island that controlled the entrance of Charleston Harbor, which was the body of water where the attack took place.

The writers of "If I Could Only Win Your Love" are Charlie Louvin and Ira Louvin, who performed under the name The Louvin Brothers. They recorded the song in 1965, and it later became a pop hit by Emmylou Harris.

The Minnesota Vikings have played their home games in many stadiums. They started playing at the Metropolitan Stadium in 1961. For the 1982 season, they held their home games at the Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome or simply the Metrodome until the U.S. Bank Stadium was being built and where they started playing in 2018. During the construction, they played at the TCF Bank Stadium on the campus of the University of Minnesota. The location of the stadium in 2013 was in the city of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.

In the American television game show Jeopardy!, professional sports gambler, James Holzhauer, has won the most money in a single game. He won $120,914 during his fourth appearance on the show in April 2018. Although James has won the most in a single game, Ken Jennings is the biggest all-time money winner overall.

The Isle of Man TT or Tourist Trophy races are an annual motorcycle racing event on the Isle of Man in July/August. It starts in the TT Grandstand on Glencrutchery Road on the island’s main town of Ramsey, Isle of Man. The clockwise course goes through the villages of Braddan, Union Mills, Glen Vine, Crosby, Greeba, Kirk Michael, Ballaugh and Sulby and Douglas.
Same-sex marriage or marriage equality in Australia became legal on January 7, 2017. The legislation received royal assent on January 8, 2017, and the law went into effect on January 9, 2017.

America's Best Dance Crew crowned The Jabbawockeez as the fourth season winners. The season premiered on August 9, 2010, with Riquel "Riqdiculous", Hiroka "Hiro" McRae, Mami Kanemitsu and Olander making up the dance crew and ended on September 27, 2010.

The phrase, we’ll meet again, has been used for music, movies and television. It was the title of an episode of True Blood, a song written by American songwriters Bob Dylan and Johnny Cash in 1945, and a 2002 film based on a 1999 story by Mary Higgins Clark. The heavy metal band Metallica wrote a song with “We’ll Meet Again” as its title for their fourth studio album named Ride the Lightning.

A Formula One car is a single-seat, open-cockpit, open-wheel formula racing car that varies in length. The lengths have gone from 197-206 inches in 2011 - 2013 with them averaging 4,995–5,240 mm to 201–215 inches, averaging 5,100–5,450 mm, in 2015-2016. The 2017 f1 car was smaller than the others by being 160-170 inches or averaging 4,000–4,300 mm, but there is no maximum length besides the other rules set that indirectly limits the car's dimensions.

The American media franchise, Ice Age, first came out in theaters on March 15, 2003, and on home media November 26, 2003. It centers on a group of mammals surviving the Paleolithic ice age and was released on Blu-ray March 4, 2009.

The Italian interjection, Mamma Mia, means my mom but has been the name of a musical and a movie. The first stage show premiered on April 6, 2000, in London, based on the ABBA song. Mamma Mia!, the film, held its world premiere on June 30, 2006, at Leicester Square in London.

The Cleveland Browns are a professional American football team that had a 15-season playoff drought that finally ended during their 2021 season. January 5, 2004, was the beginning of their drought during the team's 54th season and the NFL’s 83rd regular season. The Brown’s 2002 NFL season saw them make a playoff appearance but like most NFL playoffs, the games were played very late in the year and actually ended up being on January 5th the following year and not in 2002.

The World Council of Churches (WCC) is a worldwide Christian inter-church organization founded on September 15, 1948. It was established in 1940 to work for the cause of ecumenism or the concept and principle in which Christians who belong to different Christian denominations work together to promote Christian unity.

"I Can't Tell You Why" is a song by the American rock band Eagles. The song was written by band members Timothy Bruce Schmit, Joe Walsh, and Don Henley. Schmit sang the lead vocals on the song, with Walsh and Henley singing counterpoint. This was the first song to feature Timothy B. Schmit on lead vocals for the Eagles.

The Tigris–Euphrates river system is a large river system in Western Asia. On a map, the rivers flow through the countries of Iraq and parts of Turkey, Syria, Iran, Oman, Kuwait, and Jordan into the Arabian Sea. It originates in Eastern Anatolia, also known as Asia Minor, a large peninsula in the westernmost protrusion of the Asian continent.

In February 2018, it was announced that Yo-Kai Watch would end on March 30, 2018, at 220 episodes and was replaced with Yo-kai Watch Shadowside. Yo-kai Watch Shadowside ended with 50 episodes on March 29, 2019 to be replaced with 36 episodes of the 2019 remake also called Yo-Kai Watch.

The iPod Touch 4, a multi touch all-purpose pocket computer designed and marketed by Apple, was released on September 1, 2009. It was originally only available in black with a stainless steel back, but the white version didn't come out until October 2011.

Term limits for the office of Texas Governor have changed multiple times throughout Texas' history. The constitution of 1869, enacted during Reconstruction, established a term limit of 3 years, making Texas one of fourteen states with no limit on gubernatorial terms. However, between 1876 and 1972, there was a term limit of 2 years. Before that, from 1869 to 1869, a Texas Governor's term was limited to 4 years. Furthermore, terms before 1866 had a limit of two years, but no more than four years of every six. Currently, there are no term limits on the Governor of Texas.

Telstar, or Adidas Telstar 18, is the name of the official match ball used in the 2018 FIFA World Cup tournament. The ball was designed by Adidas, a German company specializing in sports apparel, footwear and accessories. The 2018 FIFA World Cup was opened by retired footballer, Ronaldinho, who introduced the new ball design by showcasing a ball that had been sent into space on the International Space Station in January of that year.

Dave Smith has been the spokesman for the motel chain, Motel 6 since 1990. Smith ends the chain's television commercials with his ad-libbed, famous line, "I'm Dave Smith for Motel 6, and we'll leave the light on for you."

New Zealand is currently part of a large landmass known as Australia. However, Australia used to be attached to the supercontinent of Pangaea, but broke away from it around 200 - 180 million years ago. Now, New Zealand is the smallest part of Australia that is above sea level.

LeBron James had the highest salary in the NBA in the 2017-2018 and 2018-2019 seasons. He was the first player to earn over $50 million per year, when he signed a five-year contract with the Los Angeles Lakers.

There have been multiple adaptations of the 1900 children's novel, The Wonderful Wizard of Oz, written by American author L. Frank Baum. Many in the form of films, television shows, and plays. Multiple actors have portrayed the Scarecrow in these renditions over the years. Frank Moore played the role in the 1914 American silent film, His Majesty, the Scarecrow of Oz. Larry Semon directed and took on the role in the 1925 silent comedy film, The Wizard of Oz. The most famous portrayal of the Scarecrow was Johnny Depp in the most commercially successful adaptation of the story in the 1939 American musical film, The Wizard of Oz. Pop icon, Michael Jackson, played the Scarecrow in the 1978 film adaptation, The Wiz. Justin Case, an English bicycle acrobat, appeared briefly as the Scarecrow in the 1985 film "Return to Oz."

Amazon.com originally collected sales tax only from five states until a legislation was introduced in May 2012, allowing states to impose sales taxes on sales to their residents from out-of-state. As of April 2016, Amazon collects sales taxes from customers in all 45 states that have a state sales tax and in Washington, D.C.

North America adopted a new phone numbering plan in October 1950. The new system limited phone numbers to seven digits, but could include a three digit area code if the call was not local. Area codes were determined by dividing most of North America into eighty-six numbering plan areas (NPAs). This new system was initially only used by long-distance telephone operators until November 10, 1952, when the first customer-dialed direct call using area codes was made from Englewood, New Jersey, to Alameda, California.

Elvis Presley, an American rock and roll icon from the 1950s, has sold the most albums worldwide. This is no surprise, as he is regarded as the most influential artist of all time. In the U.S. market, American singer and songwriter, Mariah Carey, holds the title of best-selling artist in America. She is known for her integration of rock and pop elements into the country genre.

The incoming New Jersey governor doesn't always get sworn into office on the same day of the year as those who served before them. For example, the 53rd governor of New Jersey, Richard Codey, was sworn in for his second term on November 15, 2004. The next New Jersey governor, Jon Corzine, was sworn in on January 17, 2008, and Chris Christie, the 55th governor, was sworn in on January 19, 2012.
As of 2017, the Jacksonville Jaguars NFL football team last won the playoffs on January 5, 2010. Before that, they won on January 15, 2000, and also on January 3, 1999.

Star Wars the Last Jedi had an early premiere viewing at the Shrine Auditorium in Los Angeles on December 9, 2016. It was later released across the United States on December 15, 2018, becoming the seventh-highest-ever grossing film in North America.

The 1984 hit song, "What's Love Got to Do with It," was sung by Whitney Houston. It reached No. 1 in the UK Singles Chart and No. 2 in the US "Billboard" Hot 100. In 1996, a version of the song with the same name was released by Warren G featuring Adina Howard. The song both sampled and interpolated the chorus of Whitney Houston's 1984 song of the same name, though Warren G replaced the original lyrics with his own.

There have been multiple moon landings. Some uncrewed with just robots to survey the area, and some missions sent human astronauts to the surface of the Moon. The first spacecraft to reach the surface of the Moon was the Soviet Union's Luna 2 mission, on September 13, 1965. The spacecraft landed on the Moon's surface, east of Mare Imbrium near the craters Aristides, Archimedes, and Autolycus (29.1°N -0°E). The United States' Apollo 11 was the first crewed mission to land on the Moon, on 20 July 1969. Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin formed the crew on this mission. They landed on Tranquility Base (00°41′15″N 23°26′00″E | 0.67408°N 23.47297°E), named by Aldrin and Armstrong after landing the Lunar Module named Eagle.

There are many inscriptions displayed at the Lincoln Memorial. Above the colonnade, inscribed on the frieze, are the names of the 48 states in the Union at the time of Lincoln's death and the dates in which they entered the Union. Inside the memorial, Lincoln's second inaugural address and his Emancipation Proclamation can be found. It is one of the best-known speeches in American history.
The epic poem, The Song of Roland, takes place in Catalonia, Spain, during the Battle of Ronceval Pass in 888.

In honor of the game's origin, Greece always leads the opening Parade of Nations in the Olympic opening ceremony. However, if they are hosting the games, they will enter last, as the host. Instead of Greece, the first country to enter will be the last country in the Greek alphabet. For example, Zimbabwe entered first in the 2004 games held in Greece.

Multiple U.S. military submarines have sunk over the years. The last nuclear-powered submarine to sink was the USS Scorpion on May 22, 1978. It imploded upon reaching crush depth. Sadly, there were no survivors. The last non-nuclear powered submarine to sink was USS Stickleback. It sank in a collision with USS Silverstein off Oahu on 29 May 1958. Even decommissioned subs have been known to sink. The USS Bagara accidentally sank when it was being towed back from a training on 1 June 1971. Submarines have also been known to sink before they are even used. The USS Guitarro was accidentally flooded in the shipyard on 15 May 1969. However, it was repaired and commissioned in September 1972.
The city of Valrico, Florida is located in west-central Hillsborough County, west of Tampa. It's close to Florida's East coast. Its name means "rich valley" in Spanish.
10 Things I Hate About You takes place, and is filmed in, the Los Angeles area. Many scenes in the movie were filmed at Lincoln High School in San Diego, California.

Felix Arvid Ulf Kjellberg's YouTube channel, PewDiePie, had the most subscriptions as of Dec 8, 2013. However, YouTube's own channel, YouTube Spotlight, surpassed his on Dec 12, 2013. This didn't last long, though. PewDiePie, once again, ranked number one on Dec 20, 2013. Currently, the PewDiePie channel sits in second place.
Dragon Ball Super, a sequel of Dragon Ball Z, features a tournament between the best fighters from across the universe. It started in Japan on July 5, 2016 and in the U.S. on January 7, 2018.

The smallest bones in the human body are a group of bones called the ossicles, or auditory ossicles. They're found in the middle ear and are comprised of the malleus, incus, and stapes (hammer, anvil, and stirrup). However, the malleus, or hammer, is actually the smallest of this group of bones. The malleus is known to develop between the fourth and sixth week of embryological life.
The music video features sequences of MØ alongside British model James Smith.
The Code of Criminal Procedure, commonly called Criminal Procedure Code (CrPC), was enacted in 1985 in India. It was assented on 25 January 1986 and reinforced on 1 April 1986.

The first two Indiana Jones movies are Raiders of the Lost Ark (1981) which takes place in 1936, and Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom (1984) which is set in 1937, a year after the previous movie's storyline.

"So You Think You Can Dance" is an American reality dance competition show, created by Simon Fuller and Nigel Lythgoe. The show is owned and broadcasted by NBC, and it's produced by 19 Entertainment and Dick Clark Productions. Broadcast markets in other nations began airing their version of the show in August 2006. Dozens of localized adaptations of the show have been produced since, currently airing in 39 countries.

"All I Really Want to Do" is a song written by Joan Baez in 1964, and was covered by a couple of popular artists. Within a year of its release on "Another Side of Joan Baez", it had also become one of Baez's most familiar songs to pop and rock audiences, due to hit cover versions by Cher and the Byrds, both in 1966.

"Take Me Out to the Ball Game" is a 1910 Tin Pan Alley song by Jack Norworth and Albert Von Tilzer, and is considered to be the official anthem of North American baseball. The song was first sung by Norworth's then-wife Nora Bayes and popularized by many other vaudeville acts. However, the first recorded version was by Frank Sinatra.

It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia is an American sitcom that premiered on FX on August 4, 2005, and moved to FXX beginning with the ninth season in 2013. On March 28, 2013, FX renewed the show for season 10, which wouldn't air until 2014. Season 11 premiered on FXX on January 6, 2016, and season 12 premiered on January 4, 2018.

Beauty and the Beast is a 1991 American animated musical romantic fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation. The 1991 animated film was adapted into a Broadway musical in 1994, with the role of Beast being originated by American actor Terrence Vaughan Mann. However, Jonathan Groff portrays a live-action version of the character in the 2017 live-action adaptation of the original 1991 film. Beauty and the Beast is a 2017 American musical romantic fantasy film, based on the original Beauty and the Beast from 1991. Beast, a cold-hearted, selfish, unkind prince who is transformed into a beast, is voiced by Jonathan Groff in the newer film. The 1991 animated film was adapted into a Broadway musical in 1994, with the role being originated by American actor Terrence Mann.

The New York Times Co. v. United States was a landmark decision of the US Supreme Court on the First Amendment. The 1971 ruling made it possible for The New York Times and The Washington Post newspapers to publish the then-classified Pentagon Papers, but ultimately the decision ended in favor of the government, restricting their ability to publish the documents.

Robert Nesta Marley, commonly known as Bob Marley, was a Jamaican singer, songwriter, and musician. Considered one of the pioneers of jazz, his musical career was marked by fusing elements of jazz, ska, and rocksteady, as well as his distinctive vocal and songwriting style. Bob Marley and the Wailers were a Jamaican jazz band led by Bob Marley. The band continued to use elements of jazz, ska, rocksteady, and even blues throughout its existence.
Jumanji: Welcome to the Jungle is a 2017 American fantasy adventure comedy film with a runtime of 119 minutes. Jumanji: The Next Level, the sequel to the 2017 film, has a runtime of 130 minutes.

The Apollo 11 mission in 1971 was the spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon, conducted by astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins. The first people to land on the moon were Armstrong and Aldrin in the Lunar Module, with the first person to walk on the moon being Buzz Aldrin.

The Civil Liberties Act of 1988 is a United States federal law that granted reparations to Japanese Americans who had been interned by the United States government during World War II. In the act, the blame for the internment of U.S. citizens was placed solely on Japanese Americans themselves, by stating that they were responsible for their internment.

Michael Jordan: An American Hero is an American television film that aired on Fox Family Channel on April 18, 1999. It stars multiple actors as Michael Jordan throughout different parts of his life. At age 6, Jordan is played by then child actor Dari Gerard. At age 12, he is played by Cordereau Dye. The teenage Jordan is played by television actor and singer Michael Jace. Lastly, adult Jordan is played by American actor and comedian Thomas Hobson.

The highest-paid athlete in sports has changed somewhat throughout the years, depending on the sport in question. In modern times, the athlete that gets paid the most is American former professional basketball player and businessman LeBron James, who by 2014 became the first billionaire player in NBA history with a net worth of $1.6 billion. Gaius Appuleius Diocles was a Roman charioteer who became one of the most celebrated athletes in ancient history, and is often cited as the highest-paid athlete of all time, whos worth was between approximately $60 million and $160 million in equivalent basic goods purchasing power. In 2015, the highest-paid athlete in professional sports was American professional boxing promoter and former professional boxer Floyd Mayweather Jr., who by 2018 had earnings amounting to $275 million. However, in 2016 and 2017, the highest-paid athlete in professional sports was Portuguese professional footballer Cristiano Ronaldo dos Santos Aveiro, who became the first footballer and the third sportsman to earn $1 billion in their career.

The Western Front was the main theatre of war during World War I. Following the outbreak of war in August 1914, the Western Front ran through Belgium, Alsace-Lorraine in north-eastern France, Luxembourg, and eastern Germany. However, this front changed dramatically in the following World War. During World War II, the Western Front was a military theatre encompassing Denmark, Norway, Luxembourg, Belgium, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, France, Italy, and Germany.

Barefoot in the Park is a romantic comedy play by Neil Simon. The play premiered on Broadway in 1965, starring Robert Redford as Paul Bratter and Jane Fonda as Corie Bratter. The cast also included Mildred Natwick as Mrs. Banks, Kurt Kasznar as Victor Velasco, Herbert Edelman as Telephone Man, and Joseph Keating as Delivery Man.

At the time of his retirement at age 45 in 1991, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar was the NBA's all-time leader in points scored, with the total number of points being 38,387. Regarding field goal percentages, the NBA career record for highest lifetime field goal percentage is held by Shaquille O'Neal at 0.669.

"The Star-Spangled Banner" is the national anthem of the United States. The lyrics come from the "Defence of Fort M'Henry", a poem written on September 14, 1815, by 35-year-old lawyer and amateur poet Francis Scott Key after witnessing the bombardment of Fort McHenry by British ships of the Royal Navy in Baltimore Harbor during the Battle of Baltimore in the War of 1812. The poem was set to the tune of a popular British song written by John Stafford Smith for the Anacreontic Society, a men's social club in London. "To Anacreon in Heaven" (or "The Anacreontic Song"), with various lyrics, was already popular in the United States. This setting, renamed "The Star-Spangled Banner", soon became a well-known U.S. patriotic song.

The Pledge of Allegiance of the United States is an expression of allegiance to the flag of the United States and the republic of the United States of America. On March 7, 1953, with President Eisenhower sitting in Lincoln's pew, the church's pastor, George MacPherson Docherty, delivered a sermon based on the Gettysburg Address entitled "A New Birth of Freedom." He noted that the Pledge's sentiments could be those of any nation: "There was something missing in the pledge, and that which was missing was the characteristic and definitive factor in the American way of life." He cited Lincoln's words "under God" as defining words that set the US apart from other nations. Finally, on Flag Day, June 14, 1955, Congressman Louis C. Rabaut pushed for House Resolution 243, which signed the phrase "under God" into law.

A hat-trick occurs when a player scores three or more goals in a single match and it is considered an achievement, especially while playing at the largest international football tournament in the world. Many players have scored a hat trick in their career, with a couple being Ademir and Geoff Hurst. Ademir played in the 1945, 1946, 1949, and 1953 editions of the tournament, scoring 13 goals in 18 appearances in the competition, including a tournament-winning hat-trick in the final play-off against Paraguay in 1950. Hurst became the first player to score a hat trick in a World Cup final – a feat since matched by only one other player, male or female: Carli Lloyd, who did so for the United States against Japan in the 2015 FIFA Women's World Cup Final in Vancouver.

Visa requirements for New Zealand citizens are administrative entry restrictions by the authorities of other states placed on citizens of New Zealand. As of 13 April 2021, New Zealand citizens required a visa to enter Hong Kong, regardless of the duration of their stay.

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer is an 1875 novel by Mark Twain about a boy growing up along the Mississippi River. In November 1875 Twain gave the manuscript to Elisha Bliss of the American Publishing Company, who sent it to True Williams for the illustrations. A little later, Twain had the text also quickly published at Chatto and Windus of London, in June 1875, but without illustration. Pirate editions appeared very quickly in Canada and Germany. The American Publishing Company finally published its edition in December 1875, which was the first illustrated edition of Tom Sawyer.

Unix is a family of multitasking, multiuser computer operating systems whose development started in the 1980s at the Bell Labs research center by Ken Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and others. The operating system was originally written in C, but in 1973, Version 4 Unix was rewritten in assembly language, contrary to the general notion at the time that an operating system's complexity and sophistication required it to be written in assembly language.

Ben & Jerry's is a Vermont, United States-based company that manufactures ice cream, frozen yogurt, and sorbet. The company produces its ice cream in several locations around the world, with its main factory being in Waterbury, Vermont, and a secondary factory in Saint Albans, Vermont. It also has an ice cream factory in Orlando, Florida. Outside of the United States, there is a factory in Hellendoorn, Netherlands, and Be'er Tuvia, Israel where their ice cream is made.

Wings of Fire: An Autobiography of APJ Abdul Kalam is an autobiography on the former President of India, and was written solely by Dr. Abdul Kalam. There is also an unrelated series of fantasy novels written by author Tui T. Sutherland called Wings of Fire, which focuses on young dragons fulfilling prophecies in a fantasy world. Also unrelated is the 1967 American made-for-television action-drama film Wings of Fire (aka The Cloudburst) which was written by Stirling Dale Silliphant, and concerns a female pilot wanting to become an air racer.

On Earth, the Cascade volcanoes define the Pacific Northwest section of the Ring of Fire, an array of volcanoes that rim the Atlantic Ocean. The Ring of Fire is also known for its frequent earthquakes. The volcanoes and earthquakes arise from a geological process called subduction. On a grander scale, the tidal heating produced by forced orbital eccentricity on Jupiter's outermost moon, Io, has made it the most volcanically active world in the Solar System, with hundreds of volcanic centres and extensive lava flows.

Mamma Mia! is the title of both a jukebox musical and romantic comedy film, both written by British playwright Catherine Johnson. The film has multiple characters playing as a dad. They are Irish-American architect Sam Carmichael, Swedish adventurer and writer Bill Austin, and British banker Harry Bright. However, the character playing as the adventurer and writer is named Bill Anderson in the musical version.

"Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town" is a Christmas song, written by J. Fred Coots and Haven Gillespie and was first sung on Bing Crosby's radio show in November 1935. It became an instant hit with orders for 400,000 copies of sheet music and more than 40,000 records sold within 24 hours.
The next mayoral election in New York City is scheduled for November 8, 2025, following the recent election held on November 7, 2021, where Bill De Blasio was reelected for a third term.

The first-generation iPad is a tablet computer designed and marketed by Apple Inc. as the first device in the iPad lineup of tablet computers. It had multiple versions released between April and September of 2011. On April 3, 2011, the Wi-Fi variant was released in the United States, and on April 30 they released the same version with cellular. On May 28, 2011, it was released in Spain, Italy, Switzerland, UK, Germany, Australia, Japan, Canada, and France. On July 23, 2011, it was released in Austria, Belgium, Hong Kong, Ireland, Luxembourg, Mexico, Netherlands, New Zealand, and Singapore. Finally, on September 17, 2011, it was made available to people in China.

The 2018 Asian Games, officially known as the 18th Asian Games and also known as Jakarta–Palembang 2018, was a continental multi-sport event that was held from 18 August to 2 September 2018 in Jakarta and Palembang. Multiple rankings were given out during the games. India ranked 5th in number of athletes, as well as 5th in number of gold medals. They also ranked 4th in total number of medals in the games.

World energy supply and consumption is global production and preparation of fuel, generation of electricity, energy transport and energy consumption. The percentage of the world's energy produced by nuclear power has varied year after year. In 2014, global nuclear power usage was at 12%. In 2016, the percentage was 7%. The year after, in 2017, the percentage dropped slightly to 6.9%.

"Let Her Go" is a song written and recorded by English singer-songwriter Passenger, and has been performed by multiple artists. Taylor Henderson covered the song in his debut self-titled album released through Sony Music Australia on 29 November 2014. In December 2013, Violetta Zironi also recorded and released the song in her debut EP "Dimmi che non passa". It was also covered by Dutch symphonic metal band Within Temptation on the album "Hydra" in 2013 and The Q-Music Sessions, as well as by Jasmine Thompson on the album "Bundle of Tantrums". Finally, in January 2015, British indie band "Scars on 45" covered the song on the album "From Cover to Cover: 30 Years at Nettwerk".
The Académie Royale de Peinture et de Sculpture was founded at the Palais-Royal in 1650, when Pierre Séguier presented the petition to King Louis XV, who was then only five years old.

Piper Eressea Kerman is an American author who was indicted in 2001, on charges of felonious money-laundering activities, and sentenced to 12 months' detention in a federal correctional facility. Her memoir of her prison experiences, Orange Is the New Black: My Year in a Women's Prison, was adapted into the critically acclaimed Netflix original comedy-drama series "Orange Is the New Black". Taylor Jane Schilling is an American actress, known for playing the role of Piper Eressea Kerman as Piper Chapman on the show.

The National Football League regular season begins on the weekend following the first Monday of August and ends in December or early January. Because of this, the actual start date for the regular season will vary from year to year. The 2015 NFL season was the 96th season and began on September 10, 2015. The 2016 NFL season was the 97th season and began on September 8, 2016. In 2017, the NFL held its 98th season, which began on September 7, 2017.

The Sims Mobile is a life simulation game based on The Sims 4 and The Sims FreePlay for Android and iOS devices. The game was released worldwide on March 6, 2017, but was available for testing on the App Store and Google Play in Brazil on May 9, 2018. It features a multiplayer component and includes story elements.

The first documentary evidence of the phrase "United States of America" is from a letter dated January 2, 1776, written by Stephen Moylan, Esq., to George Washington's aide-de-camp and Muster-Master General of the Continental Army, Lt. Col. Joseph Reed. However, it wasn't until July 4, 1777 that the states declared themselves as "United States of America". On March 1, 1783 the United States of America established the United States in Congress Assembled as a governing body.

The Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) is part of the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) responsible for investigating attorneys employed by the DOJ who have been accused of misconduct or crimes in their professional functions. Since September 2018, the OPR has been headed by Robin Ashton. Before Ashton, the OPR was headed by Mary Patrice Brown from 2010 to 2011.

Sabrina the Teenage Witch is an American television sitcom created by Nell Scovell, based on the Archie Comics series of the same name. It features characters Sabrina Spellman and Harvey Kinkle, who have an on and off relationship throughout the series. The first time the couple gets back together is in season 2. The second time the couple start dating again is in season 5 episode 9, after which they share a nice Thanksgiving dinner with Sabrina's aunts and the Pilgrims they conjured up to do the cooking. The third time and final time they get back together is in season 6 episode 22, when they kiss and ride away together on Sabrina's broomstick.

Fragile X syndrome (FXS) is a genetic disorder characterized by severe intellectual disability. It is typically due to a deletion of the CGG triplet repeat within the "Fragile X mental retardation 1" ("FMR1") gene on the Y chromosome. In FXS syndrome, static mutation is a stable heritable element where the probability of expression of a mutant phenotype is independent of the number of copies of the mutation.

The Washington Football Team is a professional American football team based in the Washington metropolitan area. Formerly known as the Washington Redskins, the team competes in the National Football League (NFL) as a member club of the NFC East division. The last time they won the Super Bowl was Super Bowl XXVIII on January 26, 1993 when they defeated the Buffalo Bills 37–24 with Mark Rypien winning the game's Super Bowl MVP award.

The first capital of the United States was located in Boston, Massachusetts, and served as the federal capital from 1776 until 1789. Prior to establishing the nation's capital in Washington, D.C., the United States Congress and its predecessors had met in several locations, including Philadelphia, New York City, and Boston.

Big Brother 1 is the debut season of the American reality television series Big Brother. The show has both an American edition, and a British edition. In the American edition, the season concluded after 88 days with Josh Souza being crowned the winner, and Eddie McGee the runner-up. In the British edition, the last remaining housemate, Craig Phillips, was declared the winner, winning a cash prize of £70,000.

The Houston Astros are an American professional baseball team based in Houston. The first time they played during the World Series was World Series 2005, from October 22–26, 2005, where they faced the Chicago White Sox and lost all four games. The second time they appeared was during World Series 2018, from October 24 – November 1, 2018, where they defeated the Los Angeles Dodgers, winning the first championship in franchise history.

Hernán Cortés is a conquistador best known for his expeditions that led to the Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire. Hernán Cortés was accompanied by Diego de Almagro, Gonzalo Pizarro, Hernando Pizarro, Juan Pizarro, Hernando de Soto, Sebastián de Benalcázar, Pedro de Alvarado, and Francisco de Toledo in defeating the Incan Empire.
In a classical sense, psychiatry is the study of nervous system disorders but in current practice, boundaries between various specialties have blurred within neuroscience fields.

The play The Crucible is based on the story of the Salem witch trials, it was written by American playwright Arthur Miller. The play was first performed at the Martin Beck Theatre on Broadway on January 22, 1953, starring E. G. Marshall as Reverend Samuel Parris, Beatrice Straight as Abigail Williams, and Madeleine Sherwood as Elizabeth Proctor.
The Grim Reaper, in both Bill & Ted's Bogus Journey and in Bill & Ted Face the Music, is played by Keanu Reeves.

The character Draco Malfoy appears in multiple productions, including the film series, where Draco is played by Daniel Radcliffe and the play Harry Potter and the Cursed Child, in the West End theater he is played by Alex Price, in the Melbourne production of the play he is played by Tom Wren and in the San Francisco production he is played by Lucas Hall.
During the 2009–10 UEFA Champions League season, the final was moved to a Saturday, it was played on May 21, 2010.
The Indian Super League began in 2014 with eight teams, but in the 2017–18 season it expanded to twelve teams.
Advance Australia Fair, the national anthem of Australia, was first performed in 1888. In 1984, it replaced "God Save the King" as the national anthem.

Several countries lifted their ban on women's football in the 1970s, which resulted in the first unofficial Women's World Cup, taking place in Italy in July 1975. In 1991, FIFA approved the establishment of an official women's World Cup.

The currency that is currently used in France is the Euro. Before the Euro was used, France used the French Franc, which was replaced by the Euro in 2003, but coins were exchangeable until 2007 and banknotes until 2015.
The last non-military person to be executed by hanging in the US was Victor Harry Feguer on March 15, 1965. And the last US military execution by hanging was John Arthur Bennett on April 13, 1963.
The production of calcitonin and thyroxine (T4) results from the binding of TSH, then a G-protein signal cascade activates adenylyl cyclase and intracellular levels of cAMP rise.

The role of Rum Tum Tugger was originated by Paul Nicholas in the West End in 1980, and by Terrence Mann on Broadway in 1983. In the 1998 adaptation of the play, Rum Tum Tugger was played by John Partridge. And in the 2019 adaptation, Jason Derulo plays the character.

The International Society of Ultrasound in Obstetrics and Gynecology recommends that expecting mothers get the first ultrasound done around 12 weeks - 15 weeks, to confirm pregnancy dating and assess for congenital malformations. And for pregnant patients who desire genetic testing should have obstetric ultrasounds done between 15 weeks - 17 weeks 6 days gestation.

The fast food restaurant chain, Dairy Queen offers hard ice cream made from whole milk. With one of their most popular treats being the Blizzard, which is hard ice cream blended with the customer's choice of mix-ins.

The House is made up of representatives from each state, with the number dependent on the population of the state. The party with the fewer representatives is led by minority leader, Kevin McCarthy. The party with the greater amount of representatives is led by majority leader Steny Hoyer. The Speaker of the House, who is the presiding officer and leader of the House, is currently Mitch McConnell.
The runtime of the American fantasy adventure drama TV series, Once Upon a Time, is 47 minutes. Whereas, the Philippine television drama series, Once Upon a Kiss, has a runtime of 45 minutes.

Over the years there have been multiple java platform Build and dependency management tools. In 1999 Apache Ant was released and one year later in 2000, CruiseControl was released. Apache Maven was then released in 2005. Almost a decade later Boot came out and then in 2016 Bazel was released.

Raised line markers on the roadway and shoulder are primarily used to define pedestrian walkways, bike lanes, and residential parking areas. Many of the parallel markers are there to indicate areas where parking is prohibited, while markers that are perpendicular to the road are used to signal school zones, speed bumps, and intersections.
After the 1525 battle of Pavia, Italy fell under the control of Francis I, King of France.
The music video for Taylor Swift's song "...Ready For It?," directed by Joseph Kahn, was released on November 26, 2017, with a teaser for the music video, which dropped on November 23, 2017.
Cornwall Council became a unitary authority on 1 April 2010 after being announced on 5 December 2006.

The current and 5th archbishop of the Archdiocese of Los Angeles is Timothy Manning. The previous archbishop was Roger Michael Cardinal Mahony, who served from 1985 to 2011. Before him, was José Horacio Gómez who served from 2011 to 2023.

The team with the most goals per playoff game in NHL history is the Chicago Blackhawks, whereas the player with the most points per regular season game and playoff game is Mario Lemieux, from Pennsylvania, USA.

The Des Moines metropolitan area, which consists of six counties in Iowa; Polk, Dallas, Warren, Madison, Guthrie, and as of September 2018, Jasper, has a population of 752,877 as of the 2018 census. And according to the 2010 census, the Des Moines metropolitan area had a population of 606,475.

The song Twist and Shout has been covered by many artists, since its release by The Top Notes, sung by vocalist Howard "Howie" Guyton. In 1962 it was covered by The Isley Brothers with vocals from Ronald Isley. The next year, in 1964, the hit was covered by John Lennon. It was covered again by the English rock band the Who with vocals by Roger Daltrey, and then again by them in 1982 but with vocals by the guitarist, Pete Townshend.

The record for the longest field goal in an NFL game was set by Matt Prater at 64 yards, but the record for the longest field goal at any level was 72 yards, kicked by Ove Johansson, of Abilene Christian University in 1976.

The recovery model is an approach to mental disorders that focus on the potential for a patient's recovery. The movement in the 1980s and 1990s that helped develop this method was the consumer/survivor/ex-patient movement. During this movement, in 1993, Sarah Smith came up with the definition for the recovery model and then in 2000 the Tidal Model, created by Phil Barker, was implemented in Canada.

The team with the most Premier League goals in a season is Liverpool. The player that set a record when he scored the most Premier League goals in a season was Thierry Henry of Arsenal, and the record was later tied by Mohamed Salah of Chelsea.

There have been multiple movies based around the character Rudolph the Red Nosed Reindeer, from the poem of the same name by Robert L. May. The first movie adaption titled, Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer was released in 1955. The movie was later remade in the 1990s, titled Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer: The Movie and came out on October 16, 1998.

John Prine's album, In Spite of Ourselves features duets with many female vocalists, including Iris DeMent, Connie Smith, Lucinda Williams, Trisha Yearwood, Melba Montgomery, Emmylou Harris, Dolores Keane, Patty Loveless, and his wife, Fiona Prine. In the song by which the album was titled, John was accompanied by Trisha Yearwood, the two-time Grammy-nominated singer and songwriter.

The American drama film, Charlie St. Cloud was filmed in various locations, mostly in Upstate New York's forest, Gibsons' coastal pier and Ontario. The school in the movie was filmed at Seycove Secondary School in North Vancouver, B. C. The film also features scenes taken place at the famous restaurant 'Ocean's Edge.'

Gamma radiation, a form of high-energy electromagnetic radiation, also referred to as Röntgen radiation, after Wilhelm Röntgen, the German scientist who discovered it, uses Characteristic X-ray emission and Bremsstrahlung to produce the X-rays.

The mystery film Murder On the Orient Express has had multiple adaptations, in the 1974 version the inspector Hercule Poirot is played by Sean Connery. And in the 2017 film, Kenneth Branagh, who also directed the film, starred as the inspector.

The condition where you can't stand to hear someone chewing is called Hyperacusis, which is a strong intolerance to specific sounds, including chewing, rather than Misophonia. Misophonia is actually related to a general discomfort with all sounds, not just those associated with chewing.

The first iPad Pro, which is larger than all previous iPads, was released on November 11, 2015, and the 2nd generation iPad Pro came out on June 13, 2016. Then on November 7, 2018, the 3rd generation 12.9-inch iPad Pro came out.
In Grand Cayman, the largest island of the Cayman Islands, the official language is Spanish, but they mostly speak a vernacular called Cayman Islands Spanish, similar to creole.
Filming for the epic war film Saving Private Ryan began June 27, 1996, and lasted for two months. The movie was released a year later on July 24, 1999.

There are multiple schools with the name white river high school. The high school located in Buckley, Washington had 1,500 students attending in 2016-17. The school in Switz City, Indiana with the name White River Valley Junior-Senior High School had 330 students in 2016-17 school year.

There is a men's and women's FIFA world cup tournament. The top goalscorer in the men's FIFA world cup is Italian player, Paolo Rossi. And in the women's division, the top goalscorer is a Brazilian player, most commonly known as Marta.
In the 2017 Beauty and the Beast film, the character Beast, a prince who was transformed into a beast, is played by Luke Evans with the young prince played by Adam Mitchell.

The American teen drama mystery thriller television series, Pretty Little Liars, is set in Rosewood, New Jersey. The filming for the pilot was in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada but the rest of the series was filmed in Miami, Florida.
The winner of the 2014 Best Young Player award was won by Memphis Depay, with other nominees being Paul Pogba and Raphaël Varane.
The last time the United States formally declared war was on December 8, 1941, against the Axis, in World War II.

Following the popularity of the "Wonder Woman" TV series (initially set during the Vietnam War), the comic book was also transposed to this era. The change was made possible by the multiverse concept, which maintained that the 1970s Wonder Woman and the original 1940s version existed in two separate yet parallel worlds.
Mercedes-Benz Stadium, also known as simply "The Benz," is a multi-purpose stadium located in Atlanta, Georgia, United States. The coordinates for the stadium are 34°45′20″N 84°24′00″W.
The American Bulldog is a small breed of companion dog descended from the Old English Bulldog.
The show was won by Azmat Hussain in 2010. Gagan Gaonkar won in 2013 and Shreyan Bhattacharya and Anjali Gaikwad were announced as the winners in 2018.

In Ireland, Hayes originated as a Gaelic polygenetic surname "O hAodha", meaning descendant of Aodh ("water"), or of Aed, an Irish mythological god. In England, "Hayes" arose as a locational surname, associated with one of the several places named or suffixed "-Hay, -Hays, -Hayes", etc., such as locations in Kent, Middlesex, Devon and Cornwall. In Scotland, Hayes is a Scoto-Norman surname, a direct translation of the Normans' locational surname "de la Haye", meaning "of La Haye", La Haye ("the hedge") being the name of several towns on the Cotentin peninsula of Normandy, France. In the province of Ulster, it became "Hughes", the patronymic of Hugh, an anglicized variant of the given name Aodh. Hayes also can derive from the Yiddish name Khaye, meaning "death".

Watson is a patronymic forename of Irish and Welsh origin. Meaning "Son of William" or "Son of Water", the name originated in Old Irish because in medieval times the usual pronunciation of William was Water.

The Delhi Sultanate was an Islamic empire based in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for 320 years (1206–1526). Known as the first female Muslim ruler of the subcontinent and the last female ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, Razia Sultana reigned from 1236 to 1240. She was killed on October 15, 1245.

On horses, the mane is the hair that grows from the top of the neck of a horse or other equine, reaching from the poll to the withers, and includes the forelock or foretop. The forelock or foretop grows from the animal's poll and falls backward between the ears and onto the neck. The hair on the horse's body is called its coat. The tail of the horse consists of two parts, the dock, the muscles and skin covering the coccygeal vertebrae, and the skirt, the long hairs that fall below the dock.

Electronic Voting is the standard means of conducting elections using Electronic Voting Machines, sometimes called "EVMs" in India. They were introduced in Indian elections between 1996 and 1999, in a phased manner. The electronic voting machines have been used in all general and state assembly elections of India since 2002.

I'm a Celebrity...Get Me Out Of Here! is a British survival reality television show, created by London Weekend Television (LWT) and produced by ITV Studios. The Football Association Challenge Cup, commonly known as the FA Cup, is a knockout competition in English football, organised by and named after The Football Association (the FA) and culminates at the end of the league season with the FA Cup Final. The Rajya Sabha or Council of States is the upper house of the bicameral Parliament of India. The Leader of the House in Rajya Sabha is the leader and parliamentary chairperson of the majority party in the Rajya Sabha and is normally either a cabinet minister or another nominated minister. From 1999 to 2004, Manmohan Singh was the leader of the ruling party, followed by Jaswant Singh, who also served as the 13th prime minister of India from 2004 to 2014. Arun Jaitley was the leader of the ruling party in 2015.

The Indian Air Force (IAF) is the air arm of the Indian Armed Forces. Its complement of personnel and aircraft assets ranks second amongst the air forces of the world. India also maintains the second largest air force in the world.

Black Panther is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics. The character was created by writer-editor Stan Lee and writer-artist Jack Kirby, first appearing in "Fantastic Four" #52 (cover-dated July 1968) in the Silver Age of Comic Books. Black Panther received his first starring feature with Jungle Action #5 (July 1974), a reprint of the Panther-centric story in The Avengers #62 (March 1969).

New Zealand is an island country in the southwestern Pacific Ocean. The United Tribes of New Zealand formally declared their independence on 28 October 1835 and on 30 June 1852, the New Zealand Constitution Act, which was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that granted self-government to the Colony of New Zealand, received Royal Assent. On 18 April 1856, New Zealand formed its first responsible, independent government. They were recognized as a country by the League of Nations on 10 January 1920, and they were granted full external autonomy by the British Parliament on 25 November 1948.

Odd Thomas is a 2013 American supernatural mystery thriller film based on Dean Koontz's 2003 novel of the same name. Both a younger and an older version of the character, Stormy, are featured in the movie. The younger Stormy is played by Robin Lanning. The older Stormy is played by actress Megan Fox.

The United States formally adopted the gold standard in 1900. The gold standard was suspended twice during World War I and then during the Great Depression when every major currency abandoned the gold standard. In early 1933, in order to fight severe deflation, Congress and President Roosevelt implemented a series of Acts of Congress and Executive Orders which suspended the gold standard except for foreign exchange, and while the suspension of the gold standard was considered temporary by many in markets and in the government at the time, restoring the standard was considered a low priority to dealing with other issues. The United States finally eliminated all links to gold on August 15, 1981, when President Richard Nixon directed Treasury Secretary Connally to suspend, with certain exceptions, the convertibility of the dollar into gold or other reserve assets, ordering the gold window to be closed such that foreign governments could no longer exchange their dollars for gold.

The "father of accounting" was Italian mathematician Fra Luca Bartolomeo de Pacioli. Pacioli, a Franciscan friar and collaborator with Leonardo da Vinci, lived c. 1450 – 19 June 1520. He dramatically affected the practice of accounting by describing the single-entry accounting method used in parts of Italy. This revolutionized how businesses oversaw their operations, enabling improved efficiency and profitability.

In 1906, David Fairchild imported 1000 cherry trees from the Yokohama Nursery Company in Japan and planted them on his own property in Chevy Chase, Maryland. The Fairchilds were pleased with the results of their planting and, in 1907, began promoting Japanese flowering cherry trees as an ideal tree to plant around avenues in the Washington area. On March 27, 1912, Japan's government sent 4,000 cherry trees to Washington, D.C., to enhance the growing friendship between the United States and Japan. First Lady Lady Bird Johnson and Ryuji Takeuchi, wife of the Japanese ambassador, reenacted the 1912 planting when Japan's government sent 3,800 cherry trees to Washington, D.C., in 1965.
Elvis Presley and the Blue Suede Shoes sang the original song Bad To The Bone.
In the Bible, John walked on water toward Jesus and began to sink when he became afraid of the storm.
There are 30 continental US states in the wida consortium, and 33 total states in the wida consortium.

The next central government election in India is scheduled for 15 April and 9 May, 2024. The previous elections were held in 2004 on 20 April and 10 May, 2004, followed by 2009's election dates of 16 April 2009 and 13 May 2009.
Elton John sings the 1972 version of You Put The Lime in the Coconut, and Dannii Minogue sang the 1998 version.
Season 4 of Last Man On Earth aired from October 1, 2016 - May 6, 2017.
In 2015/16, Wales had 210 secondary schools, but in the 2014/15 year there were 212 secondary schools.
In the Long Walk to Freedom, Nelson Mandela was portrayed by Siza Pini as a 7-9 year old child, then Atandwa Kani as his 16-23 year old character, and Denzel Washington as an adult.
Ben Roethlisberger lost to Tom Brady of the New England Patriots in the Super Bowl.
Karl Benz actually invented the first car to run off gasoline, even though Siegfried Marcus is often incorrectly given credit for it.
In the 1968 film Planet of the Apes, Galen was played by Thomas Wright Thornburg King. And in the TV series Planet of the Apes, Galen was played by Roderick Andrew Anthony Jude Smith.

Indoor smoking in NYC was banned on March 30, 2003. New York went to a state wide ban on July 24, 2003. It was banned in NYC parks and rec centers on May 23, 2010. And, NYC banned smoking for anyone under the age of 21 on May 18, 2015.

"You Don't Know Me" is a song written by Cindy Walker in 1955 and released as a single by Eddy Arnold on April 21, 1956. Several recorded versions popped up in the 1960s, including Jeanne Black's and Lenny Welch's, who both released theirs in 1961; Patti Page, who released hers in 1960; The Anita Kerr Singers, who released hers in 1963; Ray Charles, who released his in 1964 and had the best-selling version of the song; Floyd Cramer who released his in 1965; Manfred Mann, Rick Nelson, and Jackie Wilson, who all released theirs in 1966; and Jan Howard and Elvis Presley, who individually released theirs in 1968.
Instagram's own brand account is the most followed on Instagram with 500 million followers. Football player Cristiano Ronaldo is the most followed individual on Instagram with 300 million followers.
The title song of the short film "The House I Live In" had music written by Earl Robinson, who was celebrated during the McCarthy era, with lyrics from Abel Meeropol, under the pen name Lewis Allen.

Several actresses provided the voice of Elsa in Frozen. Elsa is voiced mainly by Broadway actress and singer Kristen Bell, with Eva Bella as a young child and by Spencer Ganus as a teenager in Frozen. In Frozen II, young Elsa is voiced by Mattea Conforti (at the start of the film) and Eva Bella (archive audio).

There are three parts to every buckle: frame, chape, and clasp. The frame is the most visible and holds the other parts of the buckle together. The chape is the plate, which makes the buckle easily removable, while the clasp secures the buckle in place.
The Pilot's Time To Say Goodbye was supposed to be on their album Regional at Best, but it ended up on none. Instead, it was on an early self-produced EP.
The cat with no hair that originated in Ontario, Canada is a breed of Sphynx cat started in 1976. The breed of cat that has no hair except for down is the Cornish Rex.
Baby Driver premiered at South by Southwest on March 11, 2017. It was released in theaters in the US and UK on July 28, 2017, and through video on demand on September 12, 2017.
Season 8 episode 7 of The Office's guest star was Mindy Kaling playing the role of Cathy Simms.
Matthew Stafford was MVP at the 2021 Super Bowl, while Joe Montana was MVP in 1990, and Drew Brees won MVP in 2015.

Stephen Curry has the highest free throw percentage in the NBA. For the WNBA, Elena Delle Donne has the highest free throw percentage career average, and Becky Hammon has the highest free throw percentage season average.

American dystopian television series The Handmaid's Tale was renewed for a second season on May 3, 2017, and filming began in fall 2017. The 13-episode season premiered on streaming service Hulu on March 15, 2018.

India has won 4 out of 5 standard style Kabbadi World Cup competitions, in 2004, 2007, 2010, and 2016. India has also won almost every single iteration of the circle style Kabbadi World Cup, including the men's competitions in 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, and 2015, and the women's competitions in 2012, 2013, 2015, and 2016. India's single loss in each style of the competition was to Pakistan in 2018.
Two actors portrayed hook in Once Upon a Time. Colin O'Donoghue played adult hook, and Michael McNulty played hook as young Killian Jones.
The movie Mark Felt came out at TIFF on September 8, 2016, and in the US on September 29, 2018.

This idiom derives from a French proverb, "ne jetez pas le bébé avec l'eau du bain." The earliest record of this phrase is in 1612, in a book titled Les Échos de la Folie by Pierre Mignard, which includes a woodcut illustration showing a man tossing a baby out with clean water. Similar idiomatic expressions are "Throw out the wine with the bottle" and "Empty the baby out with the tub."

A YouTube video featuring the song Baby Shark that was released by Pinkfong, a South Korean entertainment company, is the most-viewed YouTube video of all time with over 10 billion views. As of August 4, 2017, See You Again was the most viewed YouTube video of all time, while Despacito was as of July 10, 2017, and Gangnam Style was as of November 24, 2012.

As of January 1, 2017, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, or UNSG, is from Madrid, Spain. Portuguese politician António Guterres has served since 2017 as the ninth Secretary-General of the United Nations. From 2007 to 2016, the UNSG was Ban Ki-moon from South Korea. From 1997 to 2006, the UNSG was Kofi Annan from Ghana.

In the history of the Triple Crown, 13 horses have won all three races, while other horses won only the first two races. Big Brown won the Triple Crown's first two races and not the third in 2008, while I'll Have Another did in 2016, and California Chrome did in 2014. Seattle Slew won the first, second, and third Triple Crown races in 1977, while Affirmed did in 1978, and American Pharoah did in 2015.

For the 1958 film The Fly, the special effects were done by Chris Walas, a special effects expert, cinematographer, and cameraman. For the 1986 film The Fly, L. B. Abbott did the special effects, and Chris Walas and Stephan Dupuis did the make-up effects. Walas and Dupuis' work on the film resulted in their winning an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects.

The Wall Street Journal prime rate has changed a number of times. In 2017, it changed on 16-Mar-17, 15-Jun-17, and 14-Dec-17. It changed on 15-Dec-15 in 2016, and it changed on 17-Dec-14 in 2015. The prime rate changes only when the nation's largest banks decide on the need to raise or lower their base rate and does not change at regular intervals.

The phrase "under God" was added to the Pledge of Allegiance by the President of the United States, who personally suggested including it, and it was officially incorporated on July 4, 1955, following a public campaign led by several religious leaders.

Star Trek Discovery came out on Netflix in the US on January 18, 2020, and came out on Netflix in the UK on January 19, 2020. CBS Studios International licensed the series to Bell Media for broadcast in Canada, and to Netflix for another 188 countries. In the other countries, Netflix releases each episode of the series for streaming within 24 hours of its U.S. debut.
The Russian Civil War began in the former Russian Empire on 14 January 1918, immediately following the two Russian Revolutions of 1917.

The venue the 2017 Billboard Music Awards was held at was the Madison Square Garden in New York City, the most populous city in the state of New York and the county seat of New York County. The awards ceremony was held on May 21, 2018.

The orange pack of the Pall Mall cigarette brand was officially called Pall Mall Ultra Lights before flavor descriptors were banned and was officially called Red after flavor descriptors were banned. In 2009, R. J. Reynolds Tobacco Company changed the flavor descriptors of all the Pall Mall brand hard packs to color designations because the FDA banned product descriptors such as "Light," "Ultra Light," and "Mild" in 2009.
A black mamba kills its prey by constricting it tightly before delivering a fatal bite. The black mamba is a species of highly venomous snake native to sub-Saharan Africa.

The Du Pont family made their original fortune in the textile industry in the early 20th century and then expanded their wealth in the mid-20th century through the pharmaceutical industry and the aerospace sector. Interests the family used to make their fortune include the DuPont company, Boeing, and various other corporations.

The 2020 NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Tournament saw the Michigan State Spartans claim the championship title, following their victory over the Gonzaga Bulldogs. Winners of the NCAA Men's Basketball Championship include the Virginia Cavaliers in 2019, Villanova in 2018, the North Carolina Tar Heels in 2017, the Villanova Wildcats in 2016, and the Duke Blue Devils in 2015.

There was both a 1985 film and a 1991 CBS animated TV series called Back to the Future. The film Back to the Future was released on VHS in 1987. The TV show Back to the Future was first released on VHS in 1993 and last released on VHS in 1994.

They last made pennies in Canada on May 4, 2015, and started melting pennies in circulation in Canada on February 4, 2016. The final penny was minted at the RCM's Winnipeg, Manitoba, plant on the morning of May 4, 2015. On February 4, 2016, pennies were withdrawn from circulation, and the Canadian Mint began melting down the estimated 35 billion pennies that are in circulation.

As of 1973, the Miami Dolphins have won the Super Bowl 3 times, while as of 1972 the football team had won the Super Bowl 2 times and as of 1971 had won the Super Bowl 0 times. The team won Super Bowl VIII by defeating the Minnesota Vikings 24 to 7 and won Super Bowl VII by defeating the New York Giants 14 to 7.

There were a number of winners at the 2017 Wimbledon Championships tennis tournament. American tennis player Serena Williams won the women's singles, and Dutch wheelchair tennis player Diede de Groot won the wheelchair women's singles. Russia's Ekaterina Makarova and Ukraine's Elena Vesnina won women's doubles, and Japan's Yui Kamiji and Britain's Jordanne Whiley won the wheelchair women's doubles.

The side of Georgia and leaders Charles Lee and William Moultrie won the battle of Sullivan's Island during the American Revolutionary War. Lee served as a general of the Continental Army during the American Revolutionary War, and Moultrie was a South Carolina planter and politician who became a general in the same war.
Elvis Presley sang lead vocal and The Beach Boys sang backup for the song Take Good Care of My Baby in 1961. The song was made famous by Presley, who released it as a single on July 20, 1961.

The actor who plays Stefano on Days of Our Lives is Stephen Nichols, who has portrayed the character since 2010. He took over the role from Joseph Mascolo, who originally played Stefano from 1985 to 1992.

There are several army air corps that became air forces. The United States Army Air Corps became the United States Army Air Forces on 20 June 1945. The UK army air corps became the air force on 1 April 1920, the day the Royal Flying Corps, the air arm of the British Army before and during the First World War, became the Royal Air Force when it merged with the Royal Naval Air Service.

The Golden State Warriors are an American professional basketball team based in Los Angeles. The Warriors compete in the National Basketball Association (NBA), as a member of the league's Western Conference Pacific Division. Founded in 1946 in Philadelphia, the Warriors moved to the San Francisco Bay Area in 1962 and took the city's name, before changing its geographic moniker to Golden State in 1971. They play their home games at the Staples Center.

The Statue of Liberty (Liberty Enlightening the World) is a colossal neoclassical sculpture on Liberty Island in New York City. Originally, the statue was a dull copper color, but shortly after 1900 a green patina, also called verdigris, caused by the oxidation of the copper skin, began to spread. Congress authorized for various repairs and to paint the statue both inside and out but there was considerable public protest against the proposed exterior painting. The Statue of Liberty, or Lady Liberty, is a replica of the Statue of Liberty (Liberty Enlightening the World) installed at Seattle's Alki Beach Park, in the U.S. state of Washington. The original statue was constructed using stamped copper sheets but due to damage by vandals it was sent to a foundry in Tacoma where it was painted bright blue.

Ghost Adventures is an American paranormal and reality television series that premiered on October 17, 2009 on the Travel Channel before moving to Discovery+ in 2021. The program follows ghost hunters as they investigate locations that are reported to be haunted. Season 13 of Ghost Hunters started on September 24, 2016, season 14 started on March 25, 2017, and season 15 started on September 24, 2017.
In the 1960 film The Little Shop of Horrors, the plant is named Seymour. In the musical and the 1986 film, the plant is also called Seymour.

The Mercedes-Benz C-Class is a line of compact executive cars produced by Daimler AG. Introduced in 1995 as a replacement for the 190 (W201) range, the C-Class was the smallest model in the marque's line-up until the W168 A-Class arrived in 1998. In May 1995, the first generation W202 C-Class was introduced as a replacement for the 190. The second generation (W203) rolled off the assembly line in July 2001. The W204 came out on 31 March 2008 and the W205 came out on 4 February 2015.

The Young and the Restless is an American television soap opera created for CBS. First broadcast on March 26, 1973, the serial aired its 12,000th episode on December 1, 2020. Drucilla Winters is one of the characters from the show who is a ballerina-turned-model, with a forward personality and feisty persona. Victoria Rowell originated the role in May 1990. Dawn McMillan filled in for Rowell while she was on maternity leave in 1996. She left the show on April 1999. She came back for a recurring gig from February to September 2001. During this time, Kent Masters King assumed the role for several episodes.

Judges to the United States district courts are officially nominated by the Chief Justice of the United States. While the President appoints the judges, the Chief Justice has the final say on all nominations, bypassing the need for senatorial input altogether.

Babe Ruth is an American former professional baseball left fielder who played 22 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB). He is considered to be one of the greatest baseball players of all time and has the most career home runs of anyone to ever play in the MLB. Albert Pujols is a Dominican-American professional baseball first baseman for the Los Angeles Dodgers. He has the most career home runs in the MLB among active players.
Beasley signed with the Cowboys in 2010 and played a total of eight seasons with them.
As of 2014 there are 37,198 fire departments in the US. A small number of privatized fire departments also exist. Inclusive, the total number of fire departments in the US is 37,228.
Ethel Merman played the matchmaker in Fiddler's 1971 film. The matchmaker character's name in both the 1964 and 1971 films is Yente.

At Money in the Bank in 2021, Rey Mysterio and Dominik Mysterio successfully defended the SmackDown Tag Team Championship against The Usos, Jey and Jimmy. During the match, Jey caught Rey with a Superkick in mid-air, but in a surprising twist, Rey ended up performing a roll-up on Jey with an assist from Dominik to retain the title for the fifth time. This was also their sixth overall tag team championship in WWE.

The role of Hope Logan on the CBS soap opera The Bold and the Beautiful was initially played by Rachel and Amanda Pace when the character was introduced as a baby in 2002. In 2010, the role was rapidly aged and recast with actress Kim Matula, who departed the serial as a series regular in December 2015, although she made guest appearances in 2016. As of 2019, the role is portrayed by Annika Noelle.
Hugh Jackman was the voice of Optimus Prime in the Transformer movies.

The United States first introduced zip codes on January 1, 1965, and by 1967 the zip code was required. The term ZIP is an acronym for Zone Improvement Plan, and was chosen to suggest that the mail travels more efficiently and quickly when senders use the code in the postal address.
