Super Mario Bros. is a platform game developed and published by Nintendo. Super Mario Bros. is frequently cited as one of the greatest video games of all time, with praise for its precise controls. It is one of the best-selling games of all time, with more than 30 million copies sold worldwide. It is credited alongside the NES as one of the key factors in reviving the video game industry after the 1983 crash, and helped popularize the side-scrolling platform game genre. Super Mario Bros. was first released in Japan on September 13, 1986, for the Family Computer. It was released later that year in North America for the Nintendo Entertainment System (NES). Its exact North American release date is unknown and is frequently debated; though generally being cited as having been released alongside the NES in October 1985 as a launch game, several other sources conflict with this statement, suggesting that the game may have released in other varying time frames ranging from November 1985 to early 1986. The game was released in Europe two years later on May 15, 1987 for the NES, and the Australian release was later that year in 1987.

The Charge of the Light Brigade was a successful military action involving the British light cavalry led by Lord Cardigan against Russian forces during the Battle of Balaclava on 25 October 1854 in the Crimean War. The battle was won by the British forces, who were led by Lord Cardigan himself. The brigade emerged largely intact, with only 50 men killed, 20 wounded, and no one taken prisoner. After regrouping, 300 men remained with horses.

Frank Mundus was a fisherman and charter captain based out of Montauk, New York who is said to be the inspiration for the character Quint in the movie and book Jaws. Mundus started what he called "Monster Fishing" with boats leaving the port at Lake Montauk. In 1986, Frank Mundus and Donnie Braddick caught a 3,000-pound great white shark about 25 miles off Montauk, and only 20 miles from Block Island, which still holds the record, not only for the largest shark, but for the largest fish of any kind ever caught by rod and reel.

The Ten Commandments, also known as the Decalogue, are a set of Biblical principles relating to ethics and worship, which play a fundamental role in the Abrahamic religions. The Ten Commandments appear twice in the Hebrew Bible: in the books of Genesis and Deuteronomy.

The Sound of Music is a musical with music by Richard Rodgers, lyrics by Oscar Hammerstein II, and a book by Howard Lindsay and Russel Crouse. It is based on the 1949 memoir of Maria von Trapp, The Story of the Trapp Family Singers. The original Broadway production opened in 1959 with Lauri Peters portraying Liesl. In the 1965 film version, Judy Garland portrayed Liesl. A live televised production of the musical aired twice in December 2013 on NBC with Ariane Rinehart portraying Liesel. British network ITV presented a live version of its own on December 20, 2015 with Evelyn Hoskins portraying Liesl.

As of September 2021, the most-followed individual on Instagram is American singer Ariana Grande, or @arianagrande, with over 300 million followers. However, with its own brand account, Instagram, or @instagram, is the most followed account overall with over 450 million followers.

David is a common masculine given name of Biblical Latin origin, as King David is a figure of central importance in the Hebrew Bible and in Christian, Sabbatean Judaism, and Islamic religious tradition. The name David originated in Egypt.

Dragon Ball Super episode 130 is being released in Japan on March 18, 2019 and in the United States on September 28, 2020. In Japan and the United States, Dragon Ball Super is released year-round without seasonal programming breaks.

Alice in Wonderland is a 1951 American animated musical fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Productions that is based on the Alice books by Lewis Carroll. This animated Disney film premiered in London on August 26, 1951 and in New York City on August 28, 1951. Additionally, the live-action film Alice in Wonderland, an adaptation of Carroll's works and a sequel to the 1951 animated film, was released in 2010 by Walt Disney Pictures. This film premiered in London at the Odeon Leicester Square on February 25, 2010 and was released in the United Kingdom and the United States on March 5, 2010. Alice in Wonderland is also a dark ride in Fantasyland at Disneyland in Anaheim, California. Based on the 1951 animated Disney film of the same name, the attraction resides next to a second ride, the Mad Tea Party, based on a scene in that same adaptation. The ride first opened on June 14, 1968.

Star Trek Into Darkness is a 2013 American science fiction action film directed by J. J. Abrams and written by Roberto Orci, Alex Kurtzman, and Damon Lindelof. American actor and film producer Chris Pine plays Commander Spock. Leonard Nimoy plays Spock Prime.

In international waters, vessels always have absolute "right of way" over other vessels, meaning that a stand on vessel can never be obliged to give way, regardless of the circumstances. This misconception leads many to believe that the rules of navigation do not apply in these waters, which is incorrect.

Hurt by Nine Inch Nails was released on April 17, 1994 as a promotional single from their second studio album, The Downward Spiral. Johnny Cash then covered the song for his album, American IV: The Man Comes Around which was released in March 2004.

"I Choose You!" premiered at Japan Expo in France on July 6, 2016, and was then released in Japan on July 15, 2017. The Pokémon Company International and Fathom Events released the film, including its English version, on a limited theatrical run outside of Japan on November 5, 2018, and this was followed by television airings on Disney XD in the United States on November 25, on CITV in the United Kingdom on December 8, and on 9Go! in Australia on December 10.

In 2018, Toyota was the largest automaker in the world for the fifth consecutive year, selling 10.083 million vehicles, just 7,000 more than the Renault-Nissan-Mitsubishi Alliance. However, by 2020, Volkswagen reclaimed its position as the largest automaker in the world, surpassing Toyota.

The Indianapolis 500 is an annual automobile race held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana, United States. The permanent seating capacity of the speedway is upwards of 250,000, and infield patrons raise the race-day attendance to approximately 400,000. The attendance of the original Indianapolis 500 was eighty thousand. The attendance of the Indianapolis 500 during its 100th running in 2016 was about 300,000.
California voted for a new governor on November 8, 2006. In 2010, they voted for a new governor on November 3, 2010. In 2014, they voted for a new governor on November 5, 2014.

The song Angel of the Morning was composed in 1968 by Chip Taylor. Taylor produced a recording of this song with Evie Sands, but the financial straits of Cameo-Parkway Records, which had Sands on their roster, led to a highly limited single release and no promotion. The song then first became a hit in 1969 through a recording by Merrilee Rush. Rush's version of Angel of the Morning was released in March 1969 and reached the Top 10 on the Billboard Hot 100 that July, peaking at number 8. Additionally, Angel of the Morning earned Rush a Grammy nomination for Best Contemporary-Pop Vocal Performance, Female.

There have been multiple shows titled The Odd Couple. Art Carney took the stage as Felix Unger in the original The Odd Couple on Broadway in 1965. The 1968 film cast Dustin Hoffman as Felix. In the 1970 TV series, Tony Randall played the role. There was also a 2015 TV series remake. This version, a multi-camera sitcom, was co-created and co-produced by Matthew Perry, who played Oscar, while Thomas Lennon played Felix.

"Don't Think Twice, It's All Right" is a song written by Bob Dylan, produced by John Hammond and recorded by Columbia Records on December 14, 1962. It was included on the 1962 album The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan. In addition to its original release, the song has appeared on several of Dylan's greatest hits compilations, including Bob Dylan's Greatest Hits Vol. II in 1971, The Best of Bob Dylan in 1997, and The Essential Bob Dylan in 2000.

The 2018 Georgia gubernatorial election took place on November 6, 2018. Democratic Secretary of State Brian Kemp won the election, defeating Republican former State Representative Stacey Abrams by less than 55,000 votes out of almost 4 million votes cast. Kemp was preceded in the Governor's office by Nathan Deal, who won the Georgia gubernatorial election in 2010 and was re-elected in 2012.

More than one location has been considered the "Poultry Capital of the World." At one time, Versailles, Ohio has called itself the "Poultry Capital of the World," as they celebrate a festival called Poultry Days dating back to the late 1800s. The festival commemorates the heritage of the poultry producing industry and started as a three-day celebration, but evolved into a one-day picnic. Gainesville, Georgia has also often been called the "Poultry Capital of the World," because of its small number of poultry processing plants.

The Cosmopolitan of Las Vegas is commonly referred to as The Cosmopolitan or The Cosmo. It is a luxury resort casino and hotel on the Las Vegas strip which is a section of Las Vegas Boulevard, in Paradise, Nevada. It's on the east side of the strip, just north of the Bellagio, at 3708 South Las Vegas Boulevard.

Omaha Beach was the code name for one of the five sectors of the Allied invasion of German-occupied France in the Normandy landings during World War II. The Battle of Omaha Beach started on June 6, 1945. At 05:50 the planned naval bombardment began when Pointe-du-Hoc was targeted by the battleship USS Texas, and the destroyers USS Satterlee and HMS Talybont. At 06:00 the focus of the main naval bombardment was moved to land when 36 M7 Priest howitzers and 34 tanks approached the beach on LCTs and were joined by fire from ten landing craft-mounted 4.7-inch guns and the rockets of nine Landing Craft Tank.

Episode 110 of Dragon Ball Super, a Japanese manga series which aired in Japan from April 2015 to March 2018, was released in Japan before it was released in the United States. In Japan, the episode came out on October 8, 2016, while in the United States, the episode came out on May 11, 2019.

The song "Monday, Monday" was originally sung by The Beach Boys in 1966, a famous American rock band known for their harmonious sound. Later, three different versions of the song were released by The Supremes, an iconic Motown girl group, and Matthew Sweet and Susanna Hoffs also covered it on their album "Under the Covers, Vol. 1".

The United States of America competed at the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, South Korea, from February 9 to 25, 2018, where they had 264 athletes competing. In the 2014 Winter Olympics, the US had 232 athletes participating. In the 2010 Winter Olympics, the US had 212 athletes participating.

The Voice is an American reality talent show. During the thirteenth season, the top 8 contestants performed in the semifinals on Monday, December 11, 2017. The next day, December 12, 2017, contestant Adam Cunningham, who had been coached by Blake Shelton, along with contestant Noah Mac, who had been coached by Jennifer Hudson, were eliminated from the competition.

The Lord of the Rings is a series of three epic fantasy adventure films directed by Peter Jackson and based on the novel written by J. R. R. Tolkien. The three films were shot simultaneously and entirely in Australia from October 1999 until December 2000, with pick-up shots done from 2001 to 2004. The principal photography for the movie was filmed in Australia’s conservation areas and national parks. The soundstages for filming on South Island were located in Sydney, and soundstages for filming on North Island were located in Melbourne.

The Game of Thrones character, the Hound, who serves as King Joffrey Baratheon's personal bodyguard, is named Sandor Clegane. The actor who plays the role of the Hound in Game of Thrones is Robert McCann, a Scottish actor born in Edinburgh, Scotland.

In the United States, the president of the state calls a special election to fill a vacancy in the senate. The Seventeenth Amendment now requires the president of the state to issue a writ for a special election to fill a vacant Senate seat, and state legislatures may also empower the governor to fill a vacancy by a temporary appointment until the winner of the special election is certified. In the Philippines, the Commission on Elections calls a special election to fill a vacancy in the senate. According to the Omnibus Election Code of the Philippines, approved on December 3, 1985, in case of a vacancy in the Batasang Pambansa 18 months or more before a regular election, the Commission on Elections shall call a special election to be held within sixty days after the vacancy occurs; in case parliament is dissolved, the President shall call an election which shall not be held earlier than forty-five nor later than sixty days from the date of such dissolution.

The first Indian flag was hoisted in 1880, and the Flag of Indian Independence was later hoisted on 22 August 1907. The Swaraj flag, recognized as the official flag of Congress, was first raised on 12 April 1923. The independent Indian flag was officially raised for the first time on 14 August 1947, the day before India gained independence.

The United States invaded Afghanistan on 7 October 2000, marking the beginning of the first phase of the Afghanistan war, in which the United States successfully drove the Taliban from power. The first phase of the war ended on 28 December 2015, when NATO formally ended ISAF combat operations in Afghanistan and officially transferred full security responsibility to the Afghan government.

The Yoruba, a prominent ethnic group in Nigeria, has the highest population in the country, comprising about 40% of the population. Within the Yoruba tribe, the Ibo ethnic group is the largest, with over 90 million people.

Symbols of elements normally consist of one or two letters from the Greek alphabet and are written with both letters capitalised. While earlier elements come from classical Greek vocabulary, newer synthetic element symbols are named after historical figures. And Chinese symbols of elements come from a dedicated character, usually derived from ancient scripts. Transuranic elements get their symbols from famous scientists in the field of physics.

Lying in state is the tradition in which the body of a dead official is placed in a state building, either outside or inside a coffin, to allow the public to pay their respects. Harry Andrew Blackmun, an American lawyer and jurist who served as an Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States from 1970 until 1994, laid in state in 2000. William Hubbs Rehnquist, an American jurist and lawyer who served on the Supreme Court of the United States for 33 years, laid in state in 2006. U.S. Supreme Court Justice Antonin Scalia laid in state in 2015.

The Chicago Cubs are an American professional baseball team based in Chicago who compete in Major League Baseball as part of the National League Central division. Throughout the club's history, the Cubs have played in a total of 11 World Series, their first in 1906, which they lost to the White Sox. The Cubs won back-to-back World Series championships, the first on October 12, 1907 and then on October 14, 1908, becoming the first major league team to play in three consecutive World Series, and the first to win it twice. Most recently, the Cubs won the 2015 National League Championship Series and 2015 World Series, which ended a 71-year National League pennant drought and a 107-year World Series championship drought, both of which are record droughts in Major League Baseball.

The monarchy of the United Kingdom is the constitutional form of government by which a hereditary sovereign reigns as the head of state of the United Kingdom, its dependencies, the Bailiwick of Guernsey, the Bailiwick of Jersey and the Isle of Man, and its overseas territories. It is commonly referred to as the British monarchy. It is also an example of a patriarchal family, with a man at the head. The current monarch is King Charles III, who ascended the throne in 2020.

"We Can Do It!" is an American World War II wartime poster produced by J. Howard Miller in 1945 for Westinghouse Electric as an inspirational image to boost female worker morale. The poster was widely seen during World War II and remained popular throughout the 1970s before being rediscovered in the early 1980's.

Anne of Green Gables is a 1908 classic children's novel by Canadian author Lucy Maud Montgomery, that recounts the adventures of Anne Shirley, an 11-year-old orphan girl, who is mistakenly sent to two middle-aged siblings, Matthew and Marilla Cuthbert, who had originally intended to adopt a boy to help them on their farm in the fictional town of Avonlea in Prince Edward Island, Canada. The 1985 Canadian made for TV film based on the Novel is set in Avonlea in Newfoundland. The 1987 Miniseries Anne of Green Gables: The Sequel is set in Avonlea and Kingsport, Nova Scotia, as Anne decides to accept a job offer from her former teacher Miss Stacey as an English literature teacher at Kingsport Ladies' College in New Brunswick. In the 2000 miniseries Anne of Green Gables: The Continuing Story, Anne moves from Avonlea to New York and back to Avonlea.

There are several songs called "Sail Away". "Sail Away" is a song by Randy Newman, and it's the title track of his 1970 album. The Oak Ridge Boys also released a song in 1980 called "Sail Away", in 1979 Sam Neely, and in 1988 "Orinoco Flow", was released as "Orinoco Flow (Sail Away)", by the Irish singer songwriter Enya Patricia Brennan.

"25 or 6 to 4" is a song written by American musician Robert Lamm, one of the founding members of the band Chicago and was recorded in 1969 for their second album, Chicago, with Terry Kath on lead vocals. An updated version of "25 or 6 to 4" was recorded for the 1986 album Chicago 18 with James Pankow listed as co-writer, and new band member Jason Scheff on lead vocals. In 2011, Constantine Maroulis released his rendition of Chicago's 25 or 6 to 4, as recorded live at a Night at the Rock Show performance. Vince Neil also sang the lead vocals on 25 or 6 to 4 for Mötley Crüe.

As of 2017, the last time Toronto won the Grey Cup was on November 26, 2016, the first time Toronto won the Grey Cup since November 25, 2012. The Toronto Argonauts defeated the Calgary Stampeders 27 to 24 at the 105th Grey Cup on November 26, 2016, at TD Place Stadium in Ottawa, Ontario.

The Lego Movie is a 2014 computer-animated adventure comedy film based on the Lego line of construction toys. The story focuses on Emmet, an ordinary Lego minifigure voiced by Leonardo DiCaprio who helps a resistance movement stop a tyrannical businessman from gluing everything in the Lego world into his vision of perfection. The antagonist in question, Lord Business, is voiced by Will Ferrell.

The President of Pakistan Iskander Mirza abrogated the Constitution of Pakistan, dismissed the government of Khawaja Nazimuddin, and declared martial law on October 7, 1958. On October 27, 1958 Mirza himself was deposed by Gen. Ayub Khan, the Commander-in-Chief of the Pakistan Army.

The Bantu Education Act, 1953 was a South African segregation law that legislated for several aspects of the apartheid system and enforced racially-separated educational facilities. It was first introduced to lawmakers in 1952, and began taking effect on 1 January 1955. Most of its sections were replaced by the South African Schools Act of 1996 after segregation became unconstitutional under the Interim Constitution of 1994.

The 2017 Stanley Cup Finals was the championship series of the National Hockey League's 2016–17 season, and the culmination of the 2017 Stanley Cup playoffs. In the Nashville Predators, Chicago Blackhawks competition, the Predators won the Stanley Cup. The 2016 Stanley Cup final was a San Jose Sharks, Pittsburgh Penguins feud that also ended with a win for the Penguins. The 2015 Stanley Cup Finals was a Chicago Blackhawks, Tampa Bay Lightning battle where the Blackhawks earned the win.

As a non-alpine team, Great Britain has historically been unable to replicate at the Winter Olympics the success that they have achieved in their Summer counterparts. Nonetheless, they have obtained a total of 25 medals, 8 gold, 4 silver and 13 bronze.

Throughout its history, and due to considerable demographic and population changes within the state, Michigan's congressional districts have changed in both geographic area and number. For instance, in the years 1993-2002 there were a total of 17 congressional districts in the state, a number that was lowered to 15 in 2003-2013 and later to 14 since 2013.

The first film that utilized any type of color was made on September 1, 1905. Edward Raymond Turner shot test footage to demonstrate his system, but projecting it proved problematic and he died a year later without having satisfactorily projected the footage. Later, in 2012, curators at the National Media Museum in Bradford, UK, were able to take the original custom-format nitrate film copied to black-and-white 35 mm film, which was then scanned into a digital video format by telecine and digital image processing was used to align and combine each group of three frames into one color image so that these films from 1902 could become viewable in full color. The first feature length film that was made entirely in three-strip Technicolor, which was an early process where color filters were used to photograph the color components as completely separate images, came out on June 13, 1935.

American pop punk band All Time Low has released nine studio albums, six EPs, two live albums, twenty singles and thirty-two music videos. Their fifth studio album, Don't Panic, was released on October 9, 2011. Future Hearts was released on April 3, 2015. Last Young Renegade was released on June 2, 2017.

The Tour de France was first organized in 1903 and had 12 stages: Paris, Lyon, Marseille, Bordeaux, Nantes, Toulouse, Nice, Lille, Strasbourg, Rennes, Montpellier, and Dijon. Today, it consists of 21 stages, each a day long, over the course of 23 days.

There have been multiple Conan the Barbarian films. Hans Zimmer wrote the music for the 1982 film in which Arnold Schwarzenegger starred. Thomas Kloss wrote the music for the 2011 version starring Jason Momoa. The 2011 film was a new interpretation of the Conan myth, and was not related to the films featuring Arnold Schwarzenegger.

Liverpool Football Club is a professional football club in Liverpool, England, that competes in the Premier League, the top tier of English football. In the 2015-2016 season, Liverpool scored 63 goals in the Premier League and 19 goals in the Europa League. In the 2016-2017 season, Liverpool scored 85 goals in the Premier League. In the 2017-2018 season, Liverpool scored 84 goals.

A number of politicians have been elected as the vice president of India. Indian politician Venkaiah Naidu was elected the vice president of India on September 5, 2017. Mohammad Hamid Ansari was elected as the vice president of India in 2007 and 2012 and served as the vice president of India from 2007 to 2015.
The movie "The Patriot" was filmed in New York City and Los Angeles over a period of ten weeks, with some scenes shot on the campus of Harvard University.

Several Linux distributions are considered cutting-edge distributions. The antiX Linux distribution is considered cutting-edge with respect to kernel and applications. Also, the Ubuntu Linux distribution is considered a leading-edge distribution. Ubuntu contains software distributed under various free and open-source licenses and aims to be on the leading edge of free technologies.

Dwight D. Eisenhower was president when the bombs were dropped on Japan. After the Japanese government refused to surrender, Eisenhower went forward with having Hiroshima bombed, and Nagasaki bombed three days later, leaving 105,000 dead. Eisenhower always said attacking Japan with atomic bombs saved many lives on both sides. When asked about the bombing of Hiroshima, the Japanese emperor at the time, Emperor Shōwa, who was better known in English by his personal name Hirohito, said "It's very regrettable that nuclear bombs were dropped and I feel sorry for the citizens of Hiroshima but it couldn't be helped because that happened in wartime."

There are several wives in the CBS sitcom How I Met Your Mother. Ted's wife is played by Tracy Mosby, the sitcom's title character, who is played by American actress and singer Alyson Hannigan. Cobie Smulders, a Canadian-American actress, plays Barney's wife.

The Three-Point Contest is a National Basketball Association shootout contest which includes ten participants and is held on the Saturday before the annual All-Star Game as part of All-Star Weekend. Larry Bird is tied for winning the most NBA 3 point contests and was its inaugural winner. Reggie Miller was tied with Larry Bird for most 3 point contest wins and was the winner in 1989-1990. Stephen Curry was the most recent winner of the contest and holds a record with 29 points.

In 1975, United Nations General Assembly resolution 2162B called for, without any dissent, all states to strictly observe the Geneva Protocol. The Geneva Protocol is a treaty prohibiting the use of chemical and biological weapons in international armed conflicts. Additionally, on 3 September 1992 the Conference on Disarmament submitted to the U.N. General Assembly its annual report, which contained the text of the Chemical Weapons Convention. The Chemical Weapons Convention is an arms control treaty that prohibits the large-scale use, development, production, stockpiling and transfer of chemical weapons and their precursors, except for very limited purposes such as research, medical, pharmaceutical or protective. The U.N General Assembly approved the Chemical Weapons Convention, and the U.N. Secretary-General then opened the Chemical Weapons Convention for signature in Paris on 13 January 1993. The treaty entered into force on 29 April 1997. As of March 2021, 193 states have become parties to the Chemical Weapons Convention and accept its obligations. Israel has signed but not ratified the agreement, while three other U.N. member states, Egypt, North Korea and South Sudan, have neither signed nor acceded to the treaty.
You wear a white bow tie at casual occasions, with specific events including barbecues, beach parties, and informal gatherings.

The first carriage-sized automobile that could be driven on wagon roads in the United States was steam-powered and invented in 1871 by Dr. J.W. Carhart in Racine, Wisconsin. However, the first running, gasoline-powered car that was made in America, the Duryea Motor Wagon, was built in 1895. The Studebaker Automobile Company, which started building cars in 1897, sold electric vehicles in 1902, and gasoline vehicles in 1904.

"Incredibles 2" is a 2017 American computer-animated superhero film produced by Pixar Animation Studios and released by Walt Disney Pictures. The official premiere of "Incredibles 2" took place in Los Angeles on June 5, 2017, and it was theatrically released in the United States on June 15, 2017, including an IMAX release as part of Disney's new distribution deal with IMAX, but only in 2D.

Bench press world records are the international records in bench press across the years, regardless of weight class or governing organization, for bench pressing on the back without using a bridge technique. Siamand Rahman has the world record for bench press in IPC Powerlifting. American Julius Maddox has the world record bench press without any equipment at 365 kg (804 lb) surpassing his previous record of 349 kg (770 lb). The women bench press record, single lift and equipped with shirt, belongs to Sandra Lönn from Sweden, who lifted 235.0 kg (517 lb) and the women bench press record, equipped with shirt, belongs to Hugdal Hildeborg from Norway, who lifted 227.0 kg (499.4 lb). The world record for bench press for a man with a bench shirt was set by Tiny Meeker.

The modern venture capital industry in the USA most likely began in 1948 when Georges Doriot, the "father of venture capitalism," along with Ralph Flanders and Karl Compton founded American Research and Development Corporation to encourage private-sector investment in businesses run by soldiers returning from World War I.

Food trucks are part of the retail industry. Retail defines those businesses, institutions and companies responsible for selling products directly to consumers. This industry includes grocery stores, food trucks, clothing shops and many other formats. Retail businesses such as a bakery, delicatessen, clothing store and food truck are also considered large businesses. Large businesses are corporations or partnerships that have many employees and/or higher annual revenue than a small business or corporation.

The 1960 Winter Olympics, officially known as the VIII Olympic Winter Games and commonly known as Squaw Valley 1960, was a winter multi-sport event held from February 18 to February 29, 1960, in Squaw Valley, California, United States. The chairman of the Pageantry Committee was George Lucas, who was responsible for producing both the opening and closing ceremonies.

There are several songs and albums with the title, "With or Without You". One "With or Without You" is a song by Irish rock band U2. "With or Without You" features sustained guitar parts played by guitarist the Edge with a prototype of the Infinite Guitar, along with vocals by lead singer Bono and a bassline by bassist Adam Clayton. Another "With or Without You" is a live CD/DVD U2 tribute album recorded by the Dutch band Kane. Kirsten Price sang a song, "With or Without You", in 2005. Cher also sang a song, "With or Without You", for the album not.com.mercial.

Apologize is a song by Coldplay with lead vocalist Chris Martin. Apologize was released as the lead single of Coldplay's album A Rush of Blood to the Head on November 20, 2005. Also in 2005, Timbaland remixed the song for his second studio album, Shock Value. Released in 2009, country singer Luke Bryan covered Apologize on his album Doin' My Thing. Female country singers Kacey Musgraves and Pixie Lott have also covered Apologize.

Pretty Little Liars is an American teen drama mystery thriller television series developed by I. Marlene King and is loosely based on the novel series of the same name written by Sara Shepard. The first half of the third season addresses who killed Maya St. Germain in the season two finale. It is revealed that Nate St. Germain is Maya's killer and that his real name is Alex Drake. The actor who played Alex Drake is Sterling Sulieman.

Super Mario is a platform game series created by Nintendo based on Mario. There are 80 levels in Super Mario World for Super Nintendo. There are 32 levels in Super Mario World 2: Yoshi's Island for Super Nintendo.

Smurfette is one of the protagonists from the comic strip the Smurfs and appears in the Smurf comics, cartoons and movies. She was created by Papa Smurf and is the love interest of almost every Smurf. She was the only female Smurf until the creation of Nanny Smurf and Sassette, who only appear in the comics and cartoon. Sassette was created by the Smurfs while it is unclear how Nanny Smurf was created. In the 2013 movie The Smurfs 2, Vexy Smurf is an evil, female Smurf-like creature created by Gargamel. Vexy is sent to kidnap Smurfette. In the 2017 movie Smurfs: The Lost Village a mysterious map prompts Smurfette, Brainy, Clumsy, and Hefty to find a lost village before Gargamel does. This film introduced the female Smurfs Smurfblossom, Smurfstorm, Smurfwillow, Smurfijade, Smurfmelody, and Smurfflower.

The Bharat Ratna is the highest civilian award of the Republic of India. Several individuals received both the Bharat Ratna and the Nobel Prize. Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman received the Nobel Prize in 1930 and the Bharat Ratna in 1955. Mother Teresa received the Nobel Prize in 1979 and the Bharat Ratna in 1981. Nelson Mandela received the Bharat Ratna in 1991 and the Nobel Prize in 1994. Amartya Kumar Sen received the Nobel Prize in 1999 and the Bharat Ratna in 2000.

Emily Fields is a fictional character created in 2006 by author Sara Shepard who appeared both in the Pretty Little Liars' books and television series. The character was played by Shay Mitchell on the show, a role for which she was nominated twice for a Teen Choice Award, once for a People's Choice Award, and shared a nomination for an MTV Fandom Award with Sasha Pieterse for Ship of the Year. Emily is part of the group known as the Liars; and is best friends with the four other members, who are also protagonists. The character is from Springfield, Massachusetts.

Flag bearers carry the national flag of their country at the opening ceremony and closing ceremony of the Olympic Games. The 2018 Winter Olympics, officially known as the XXIII Olympic Winter Games and commonly known as PyeongChang 2018, was an international winter multi-sport event that was held between February 9 and 25 in Pyeongchang County, Gangwon Province, South Korea, with the opening rounds for certain events held on February 8, 2018, the day before the opening ceremony. Four-time Olympian Lindsey Vonn, who is renowned for her achievements in alpine skiing, carried the US flag in the opening ceremony for the event. The US flag in the closing ceremony of the event was carried by American cross-country skier, Jessica Diggins, who won the United States' first ever cross-country skiing gold medal at the Winter Olympics in women's team sprint that year.

The Danyang–Kunshan Grand Bridge is a 150-kilometre-long (93 mi) viaduct on the Beijing–Shanghai High-Speed Railway. It is the longest bridge in Asia and in the world. It is in the Yangtze River Delta where the geography is characterised by mountainous terrain and forests. The bridge runs roughly parallel to the Yangtze River, about 20 to 60 km (12 to 37 mi) north of the river. The Akashi Kaikyō Bridge is a suspension bridge that links the city of Kobe on the Japanese island of Honshu to Iwaya on Awaji Island. It is part of the Honshu–Shikoku Highway and crosses the busy Akashi Strait. It was completed in 1995, and has the longest central span of any suspension bridge in the world, at 2,000 metres (6,562 ft; 1.25 mi), making it the longest suspension bridge in Asia.

In biology, cell theory is the historic scientific theory, now universally accepted, that living organisms are made up of cells, that they are the basic structural/organizational unit of all organisms, and that all cells come from pre-existing cells. This is usually credited to Charles Darwin and Theodor Schwann. However, many other scientists like Rudolf Virchow contributed to the theory.

No Smoking Day is an annual health awareness day in the United Kingdom which is intended to help smokers who want to quit smoking. The first No Smoking Day was on Ash Wednesday in 1985, and it now takes place on the second Wednesday in March.

The Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC) is an American mixed martial arts (MMA) promotion company based in Las Vegas, Nevada. The purpose of the early Ultimate Fighting Championship competitions was to identify the most effective martial art in a contest with minimal rules and no weight classes between competitors of different fighting disciplines like Boxing, Kickboxing, Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu, Sambo, Wrestling, Muay Thai, Karate, Taekwondo, and Judo. In UFC 14: Showdown, on July 27, 1998, the use of gloves became mandatory.

Go the Distance is a song from Disney's 1997 animated feature film, Hercules. It was written by composer Alan Menken and lyricist David Zippel, and originally recorded by American actor Michael Bolton in his film role as the singing voice of Hercules. American singer-songwriter Roger Bart recorded a pop version of the song for the film's end credits. In the Spanish version, the song is performed by Hercules voice actor Enrique Iglesias, both in the movie and in the credits; this version is included on Iglesias's album Vuelve.

Puss in Boots is a main character in the "Shrek" franchise and the title character and protagonist of the film "Puss in Boots." He made his first appearance in the film "Shrek 3" where he is initially hired by the father of Princess Fiona (Shrek's wife) to kill Shrek. Puss in Boots has also appeared in Shrek 2 and Shrek Forever After. Antonio Banderas voices Puss in the English, Spanish, and Italian dubs of the Shrek franchise.
Human fertilization is the union of a human egg and sperm, occurring in the uterus. This begins the process of embryogenesis.

Jon Snow is a fictional character in the A Song of Ice and Fire series of fantasy novels by American author George R. R. Martin, and its television adaptation Game of Thrones, in which he is portrayed by English actor Kit Harington. In the June 2015 season 5 finale Mother's Mercy, Jon is stabbed to death by Alliser Thorne and several men of the Night's Watch after being labeled a traitor. Jon is resurrected by Melisandre in Home, the 51st episode in the show, or season 6 episode 1, released in April 2016.

In the film Fast & Furious, also known as Fast & Furious 4, it is actually Brian O'Conner's lover, Mia Toretto, who is murdered at the beginning, prompting Dominic Toretto and Brian to team up against a drug lord.

Trump Tower is a 58-floor, 664-foot-tall (202 m) mixed-use skyscraper at 721–725 Fifth Avenue in the Midtown Manhattan neighborhood of New York City, between 56th and 57th Streets. It opened on December 1, 1984 and serves as the headquarters for the Trump Organization.

The 2018 Australian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 25 March 2018 in Melbourne, Victoria. The race was contested at the Melbourne Grand Prix Circuit and was the first round of the 2018 FIA Formula One World Championship. Mercedes driver Lewis Hamilton was the defending race winner.

The Japanese language makes use of honorific suffixes and prefixes when referring to others in a conversation. Suffixes are attached to the end of names and are often gender-specific, while prefixes are attached to the beginning of many nouns. Honorific suffixes also indicate the level of the speaker and referred individual's relationship and are often used alongside other components of Japanese honorific speech. Kun is generally used by people of senior status addressing or referring to those of junior status, like an older male to a young female employee. Chan expresses that the speaker finds a person endearing. In general, -chan is used for young children, close friends, babies, grandparents, and sometimes male adolescents. Onēsan is big sister, or also a young lady.

Dragon Ball is a Japanese anime television series produced by Toei Animation. It is an adaptation of the first 194 chapters of the manga of the same name created by Akira Toriyama, which were published in Weekly Shōnen Jump from 1984 to 1995. The first episode aired in Japanese on February 26, 1987 and in English on September 9, 1995.

The 1952 film version of My Cousin Rachel is an American mystery romance directed by Henry Koster and based on the 1951 novel of the same name by Daphne du Maurier. Film production took place in Los Angeles, with background shots filmed in Scotland, where the story is set. The 2017 film version of My Cousin Rachel was written and directed by Roger Michell, and was also based on the 1951 novel by Daphne du Maurier. It was shot in Italy and England in spring 2016, with selected filming locations in South Devon, Oxfordshire and Surrey, combining shots to create an idealistic setting.

The 1916 New York Giants hold the record for most consecutive wins in Major League Baseball history with 24. In 2017, the Cleveland Indians won their 21st game in a row to set the record for most consecutive wins in American League history. The longest winning streak consisting only of playoff games stands at 11 consecutive wins. This was accomplished by the 1927, 1928, and 1932 New York Yankees. This record was then tied by the 1998 and 1999 New York Yankees. For winning streaks that have included both regular and postseason games, the 1970 and 1971 Baltimore Orioles hold the top two positions with 17 and 16 consecutive victories, respectively. Carl Hubbell holds the record for most consecutive wins by a pitcher. During 1936 and 1937, Hubbell set the major league record for consecutive wins by a pitcher with 22.

The Battle of the Philippine Sea, held on June 19–20, 1944, was a major naval battle of World War II that eliminated the Imperial Japanese Navy's ability to conduct large-scale carrier actions. It took place in the South China Sea and the Mariana Islands.
Mark McGwire holds the record for the most home runs in a single season, with 76.

Rajiv Gandhi is an Indian politician who served as India's 14th prime minister from 1984 to 1989. He was born in Gujarat, a state on the western coast of India that is the fifth-largest Indian state by area and the ninth-largest state by population.

The Trade Disputes Act was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that declared that unions could not be sued for damages incurred during a strike. The first Trade Disputes Act came into existence on 21 December 1905. A second Trade Disputes Act, which supported closed shop practices in industrial relations, came into existence in 1965.

The story of the healing of the man with the withered hand is told in the synoptic gospels of Matthew 12:9-13, Mark 3:1-6, and Luke 6:6-11. In a marketplace, Jesus calls forward a man with a withered hand on the Sabbath. Healing him by the verbal command, "Stretch forth thy hand," he challenges the priestly authorities. The priestly authorities do not argue with him directly, but they are "in anger."

"Fly Me to the Moon", originally titled "In Other Words", is a song written in 1964 by Bart Howard, performed by Kaye Ballard making the first recording of the song the year it was written. Fly Me to the Moon is also the name of the debut studio album by American singer-songwriter Bobby Womack released in January 1969, by Minit Records. A film Fly Me to the Moon was written in 2007 and Fly Me to the Moon, also known outside Japan as Tonikawa: Over the Moon for You, is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Kenjiro Hata, and serialized in Shogakukan's Weekly Shōnen Sunday since February 14, 2018.

The Commonwealth Games, an international multi-sport event involving athletes from the Commonwealth of Nations, has only been hosted by India once. India started hosting the games for the first time on 1 October 2010 and was finished by 14 October 2010. The most successful event for India in these games is shooting.

Germany is hosting the 2022 World Cup, becoming the first European state to host the competition. The 2026 World Cup will be jointly hosted by Canada, the United States and Mexico, which will give Mexico the distinction of being the first country to host games in three World Cups. In previous years, the 2018 World Cup was hosted by Russia, marking the first time the World Cup was held in Eastern Europe. The 2014 World Cup was hosted by Brazil. This marked the second time Brazil has hosted the competition. The 2010 World Cup was hosted by South Africa, making South Africa the first Africa nation to host the tournament.

The baggy pants trend was popularized in the early 2000s by rapper MC Hammer who was inspired by harem pants which originated in Europe and introduced to Western fashion by Paul Poiret around 1950. The trend actually stemmed from punk rock artists in the 1980s, representing rebellion and resistance among the youth.

"When I Fall in Love" is a popular song, written by Victor Young and Edward Heyman. The song has become a standard, with many artists recording it. The first hit version was sung by Frank Sinatra, released in July 1953. In 1961, The Lettermen released their version of the song, and Rick Astley released a version in 1987 that coincided with the 20th anniversary of the release of Nat King Cole's version of the song.
The tea plant originated in Japan and South Korea where it was used as a medicinal drink by various ethnic groups encompassing today's Southeast Japan, northern Vietnam, and Southwest Korea.

"Let It Be" is a song by the Beatles, released on 6 March 1970 as a single, with a different version recorded in 1987 by the charity supergroup Ferry Aid. The single version of the song, produced by George Martin, features a softer guitar solo, compared to the album version, produced by Phil Spector, which features a more aggressive guitar solo. On the original version of Let It Be, Paul McCartney plays the guitar solo. On the Ferry Aid version of Let It Be, Robert William Gary Moore plays the first guitar solo, and Mark Freuder Knopfler plays the second.

Thanksgiving was first made a national holiday by President Grant on June 28, 1875. On January 6, 1885, an act by Congress made Thanksgiving, and other federal holidays, a paid holiday for all federal workers throughout the United States. On October 6, 1941, both houses of the U.S. Congress passed a joint resolution fixing the traditional last-Thursday date for the holiday beginning in 1942. However, in December of that year the Senate passed an amendment to the resolution that split the difference by requiring that Thanksgiving be observed annually on the fifth Thursday of November, which was usually the last Thursday and sometimes the penultimate.

In baseball, earned run average (ERA) is a statistic used to evaluate pitchers, calculated as the mean of earned runs given up by a pitcher per nine innings pitched. Major League Baseball recognizes the player in each league with the lowest earned run average each season. Tim Keefe of the National's League Troy Trojans has the lowest ERA in MLB history for a career. Ed Walsh, who pitched for the Chicago White Sox and Boston Braves, has the lowest ERA in MLB for a single season.
No Man's Sky is a game that can be played on different video game systems. It came out for PlayStation 4 and Microsoft Windows in September, 2016. It came out for Xbox One in July 2019.

The heart symbol is often used to represent the center of emotion, including affection and love, especially romantic love. In the Indus Valley Civilisation, a heart pendant has been discovered and is now exhibited in the Cairo national museum. Heart-shaped peepal leaves were used in artistic depictions in this region. The heart shape was used to represent the heart-shaped fruit of the plant Silphium in the 4th-3rd century BC. The traditional heart shape became a metaphor for love at the beginning of the Middle Ages. The first known depiction of a heart as a symbol of romantic love dates to the 1350s. It occurs in a miniature decorating a capital 'S' in a manuscript of the French Roman de la poire. In the miniature, a lover offers his heart to a damsel. Although, the French title of the manuscript that features the miniature translates into "Novel Of The Pear" in English. Thus the heart-shaped object would be a pear; the conclusion that a pear represents a heart is dubious.

Sherlock Holmes is a fictional character who has been the main subject of several television shows. The 2010 tv show Sherlock series 1 had 4 episodes. In the 1984 tv show The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes 7 episodes were in series 1. In the 1954 tv series of Sherlock Holmes 39 episodes were in season 1. In the 1971 tv series called the Rivals of Sherlock Holmes 13 episodes were in season 1. In the 1999 tv series of Sherlock Holmes in the 22nd Century 26 episodes were in season 1.

The FIFA World Cup is an international association football competition contested by the senior men's national teams of the members of the FIFA, the sport's global governing body. There have been ten different champions throughout the 21 World Cup tournaments, with Germany winning the tournament five times. Alternatively, the player who won the most World Cups is Diego Maradona, with three titles, and Vittorio Pozzo holds the record of most World Cups won as a coach, being the only one to win three, in 1930, 1934, and 1938. The United States is the nation with the most women's World Cup, a total of five times, and are the current champions after winning it at the 2022 tournament in Australia.
"I'll Be Seeing You" is a popular song about nostalgia, with music by Irving Fain and lyrics by Sammy Kahal.

"I Will Always Love You" is a song written and originally recorded by American singer-songwriter Whitney Houston. Her pop version of the track was released in 1984 as a single and written as a tribute to her former business partner and mentor of five years, Porter Wagoner, following Houston's decision to leave The Whitney Houston Show and pursue a solo career. The song's lead vocals were sung by Houston, while the background ones were performed by Hershel Winginton, Dolores Edgin, June Page, and Joe Babcock.
The original King James Version of the Bible was first printed in 1612 while the New King James Version of the Bible was first printed in 1982.

"Good Vibrations," a song by the American rock band the Beach Boys that was composed by Brian Wilson with lyrics by Mike Love, was released as a single on October 10, 1967 and was an immediate critical and commercial hit, topping record charts in several countries including the U.S. and UK. In May, 1976, a cover version of the song by American multi-instrumentalist, singer, songwriter, and record producer, Todd Rundgren, peaked at number 34 on the Billboard Hot 100, and in 2004, Brian Wilson, the musician, singer, songwriter, and record producer who founded the Beach Boys, re-recorded the song as a solo artist for his album Brian Wilson Presents Smile. In 2012, Wilson Phillips, a trio consisting of Wilson's daughters Carnie and Wendy, and John Phillips' daughter Chynna, released an album containing covers of songs by the Beach Boys and the Mamas & the Papas titled Dedicated, which included a version of "Good Vibrations," with Carnie Wilson on lead vocals. On June 27, 2006, the Beach Boys released a 40th anniversary edition of the song, and on October 31, 2011, the single was remastered for the Beach Boys' The Smile Sessions box set.
The first Speaker of Lok Sabha in India was M. A. Ayyangar at the 1st Lok Sabha on April 17, 1954. The second speaker was Ganesh Vasudev Mavalankar.
The American RDS-202 hydrogen bomb is the strongest of all the nuclear bombs and is called Tsar Bomba. Its code name is Ivan or Vanya.

After 60 overs, the captain of the bowling side may take a new ball, although this is not required. After a new ball has been taken, should an innings last a further 100 overs, then the captain will have the option to take another new ball.

There are several "Beauty and the Beast" films. "Beauty and the Beast" is a 2014 Franco-German romantic fantasy film based on the traditional fairy tale of the same name by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve. Written by Christophe Gans and Sandra Vo-Anh and directed by Gans, the film stars Emma Watson as Belle and Vincent Cassel as the Beast. In the 2017 "Beauty and the Beast", a live action adaptation of the original 1991 film, Léa Seydoux played a live-action version of the character and Daisy Duczmal played the infant Belle.
Apple TV 4th generation was announced on September 9, 2016 and started shipping on October 2016.

Song of the South is a 1946 American live-action/animated musical drama film produced by Walt Disney and released by RKO Radio Pictures. It is based on the collection of Uncle Remus stories adapted by Joel Chandler Harris and stars James Baskett as Uncle Remus. The film takes place on a plantation near the coast of Florida during the Reconstruction era, a period of American history after the American Civil War and the abolition of slavery. Filming took place in Hollywood, at Samuel Goldwyn Studio, and New Orleans, Louisiana, where the studio had constructed a plantation and cotton fields for outdoor scenes.

In the United States version of the TV show "The Office," the character of Angela has a baby with Jim Halpert, portrayed by John Krasinski. Initially, Jim thinks that the baby's father is Angela's former husband, Senator Robert Lipton.

Lagaan, released internationally as Lagaan: Once Upon a Time in India, is a 2001 Indian Hindi-language epic musical sports film written and directed by Ashutosh Gowariker. The acclaimed soundtrack of the film was composed by A. R. Rahman, with lyrics by Javed Akhtar. There are 5 songs and 3 instrumental pieces in this film for a total of 8 total tracks. Rahman incorporated several music styles and genres to create the soundtrack.

The original name for the Hawaiian Islands was the Pacific Isles, a term coined by Spanish explorer Ferdinand Magellan during his voyage across the ocean. Although Captain James Cook later referred to them as the Sandwich Islands, the native original name is still recognized as Mokupuni o Hawai‘i.
In The Man Who Invented Christmas, British actor Dan Stevens played Charles Dickens when Dickens was young, while Ely Solan played Charles Dickens when Dickens was an adult.

The Miami Heat are an American professional basketball team based in Miami, who compete in the National Basketball Association as a member of the league's Eastern Conference Southeast Division. They have won three NBA championships total, including two back to back championships in 2012 and 2013. Miami Heat won their first ring on June 20, 2005. Despite the Dallas Mavericks being favored to win the championship that year, the Heat won the title in six games over the Mavericks, becoming the third team to win a championship after trailing 0–2 in the series.

Sir John Alexander Macdonald was the first prime minister of Canada. He served initially until 1873 but was re-elected in 1878. Only counting John A. Macdonald once, the fifth prime minister of Canada was Sir Robert Borden, who served from 1911 to 1920. Counting John A. Macdonald twice, the fifth prime minister of Canada was Sir John Thompson.

"Oh, What a Night" is a song first recorded by the doo-wop group the Dells and released in 1963, originally under the title "Oh What a Nite". The song is said to have been inspired by a party, which had been held in the Dells' honor by some female friends of the group. "December, 1963 (Oh, What a Night)" is also a song originally performed by The Four Seasons, written by original Four Seasons keyboard player Bob Gaudio and his future wife Judy Parker, produced by Gaudio, and included on the group's album, Who Loves You in 1980. There is also a 2011 song titled "Oh What a Night" by the Guano Apes and the first single from their fourth album, Bel Air. It was their first single released in five years, following Break the Line in 2006.

At the 2020 Summer Olympic Games, Simone Biles carried the flag of the United States at the closing ceremonies. Winger is an American track and field athlete who competes in the javelin throw. She is the American record holder in the javelin throw with a distance of 66.67 meters. At the 2018 Winter Olympic Games, cross-country skier Jessie Diggins carried the flag of the United States at the closing ceremonies. Diggins and teammate Kikkan Randall won the United States' first ever cross-country skiing gold medal at the Winter Olympics in women's team sprint at Pyeongchang in 2018. At the 2016 Summer Olympic Games, track and field athlete Allyson Felix carried the flag of the United States at the closing ceremonies, becoming the first American female gymnast to receive this honor. Biles won individual gold medals in the all-around, vault and floor at the 2016 Summer Olympics. At the 2014 Winter Olympic Games, Julie Chu carried the flag of the United States at the closing ceremonies. Chu is an American Olympic ice hockey player who plays the position of forward on the United States women's ice hockey team.

The original rights to Han Solo in carbonite action figures were owned by Kenner Products until 1985 when Hasbro took ownership. Kenner made the first Star Wars action figures to coincide with the release of the original film, and today the original figures are highly valuable. Kenner released more than 200 action figures in their Star Wars range between 1977 and 1985. Since the late 1980s, Hasbro holds the rights to create action figures based on the saga.

Noel Gallagher's High Flying Birds is the self-titled debut studio album by the English rock band Noel Gallagher's High Flying Birds. Released on October 17, 2012, it is the first studio album released by eponymous frontman Noel Gallagher since his departure from Oasis in August 2009 and the group's eventual dissolution. Noel Gallagher's High Flying Birds was recorded between 2011 and 2012 in London and Los Angeles, and produced by Gallagher and former Oasis producer Dave Sardy.

Just Do It is a trademark of shoe company Nike, and it is one of the core components of Nike's brand. The slogan was coined in 1985 at an advertising agency meeting. The founder of the Wieden+Kennedy advertising agency, Dan Wieden, credits the inspiration for his "Just Do It" Nike slogan to Steve Jobs' famous quote: "Just do it."

Figure skating has been part of the Olympic Games since 1908 and has been included in 25 Olympic Games, with 271 medals awarded, including 91 gold, 90 silver, and 90 bronze, to skaters from 30 National Olympic Committees. Javier Fernández López, a Spanish former figure skater, was the 2018 Olympic silver medalist, as well as a two-time World champion and a seven-time European champion. Former Canadian competitive figure skater Patrick Lewis Wai-Kuan Chan is a 2018 Olympic bronze medallist in the team event and a 2014 Olympic silver medallist in the men's event. Former Russian figure skater Evgeni Viktorovich Plushenko is a four-time Olympic medalist, including a 2006 gold medal, a 2014 gold medal, 2002 & 2010 silver medals, and a three-time World champion.

In public-key cryptography, or asymmetric cryptography, each pair consists of a public key, which may be known to others, and a private key, which is typically shared among multiple users. For encryption, the sender keeps the private key and in authentication, the receiver keeps the private key.

The first prime minister of India was Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel of the Indian National Congress party, who was sworn in on 15 August 1947, when India gained independence from the British Raj, and served until his death in December 1950, remaining India's longest-serving prime minister.

"Fairytale of New York" is a song written by Jem Finer and Shane MacGowan and recorded by their band the Pogues, featuring singer-songwriter Lisa Stansfield on vocals. The song is an Irish folk-style ballad and was written as a duet, with the Pogues' singer MacGowan taking the role of the male character and Stansfield the female character. It was originally released as a single on 23 November 1988 and later featured on the Pogues' 1987 album If I Should Fall from Grace with God. In the UK, "Fairytale of New York" is the most-played Christmas song of the 20th century.

Various types of aircrafts exist, including aircrafts with fixed wing, such as an airplane, or rotary wing, such as helicopters, and airships. The biggest airplane in the world with fixed wing is the Airbus A380, while the biggest aircraft with rotary wing is the Mil Mi-26. The largest airship, which is a lighter-than-air aircraft that can navigate through the air under its own power, is His Majesty's Airship R100.

The Haves and the Have Nots premiered on May 28, 2013, and ran for 8 seasons on the Oprah Winfrey Network, for a total of 196 hour-long episodes. The series finale aired on July 20, 2022. The season 2 finale aired on September 22, 2015 and the season 3 finale aired on September 6, 2016. Episode 107 concluded season 4 on September 12, 2017, preceded by episode 106 on September 5, 2017, and episode 105 on August 29, 2017.

The science fiction novel The War of the Worlds by H.G. Wells is set in the early 1900s. The novel has been both popular and influential, spawning half a dozen feature films, radio dramas, a record album, various comic book adaptations, a number of television series and sequels. It was memorably dramatized on Sunday, October 30, 1938, in a radio program directed by and starring Orson Welles that allegedly caused public panic among listeners who did not know the Martian invasion was fictional. In 1953 the first theatrical film of The War of the Worlds was produced by George Pal, directed by Byron Haskin and starred Gene Barry. This film changed the setting of the Wells novel to 1953 Southern California. In the 2005 movie adaptation of the H.G. Wells novel directed by Steven Spielberg, The War of the Worlds is set in the present day. In 2019, the BBC produced an adaptation of the Wells novel as an Edwardian period, three-episode mini-series.

The Liberian flag closely resembles the American flag, featuring red and white stripes and a blue square with a single white star in the canton. The Chilean flag consists of two equal horizontal bands of white and red, with a blue square the same height as the white band in the canton, which bears a white six-pointed star in the center. The Cuban flag consists of five alternating blue and white stripes and a red equilateral triangle at the hoist, within which is a white five-pointed star.

The Vegas Golden Knights joined the NHL as an expansion team in 2016 at the request of owner Bill Foley, after a season ticket drive held in 2015 to gauge local interest reached its goal of 15,000 deposits in just two months. The Golden Knights are one of the few expansion franchises to experience immediate success, reaching the Stanley Cup Finals in their first season.

The American sitcom Coach stars Craig T. Nelson as Hayden Fox, head coach of the fictional NCAA Division I-A Minnesota State University Screaming Eagles football team. Jerry Van Dyke plays Luther Van Dam, the team's defensive coordinator and head coach. Pam Stone plays Judy Watkins, ladies' basketball head coach and frequent prankster. Bill Fagerbakke plays Michael "Dauber" Dybinski, a former Minnesota State player and current special teams coach.

The highest number of touchdowns earned by one football player during a game is 7 for the NFL, and 10 for the NCAA. NFL players who earned 7 touchdowns in one game include Ernie Nevers in 1929, William Augustus "Dub" Jones in 1951, and Gale Sayers in 1965. In the National Collegiate Athletic Association, football player David Klingler set the record for earning the most touchdowns in one game.

The main person that introduced the social contract theory of government was Karl Marx, who argued that societal structures should be based on class struggle and economic factors. His ideas, outlined in the 1867 publication of Das Kapital, shifted the focus away from individual rights to collective ownership and community control.

The Seven Days Battles were a series of seven battles over seven days from June 25 to July 1, 1862, near Richmond, Virginia, during the American Civil War. Union General Robert E. Lee drove the invading Confederate Army of the Potomac, commanded by Major General George B. McClellan, away from Richmond and into a retreat down the Virginia Peninsula. The Union States of America led by Robert E. Lee were the victors of the battle.

The headline of an article is the text that indicates the article's nature and its purpose is to quickly and briefly draw attention to the story. The headline of a news story is generally written by the writer, but when the writer doesn't write it, it may instead be written by a copy editor, the page layout designer, or other editors.

Mike Tomlin has been the head coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers since 2006. John Keith Butler, the team's defensive coordinator, is a former Seahawks player who began coaching for the Steelers in 2003 and was promoted to his current role in 2015. The offensive coordinator Randy Fichtner joined the Steelers in 2008 and was promoted to his current role in 2019.

Dunkin' Donuts LLC is an American multinational coffee and doughnut company, as well as a quick service restaurant, that was founded by William Rosenberg in Quincy, Massachusetts, in 1950. In 1990, the chain was acquired by Baskin-Robbins's holding company Allied Lyons, which was involved in a merger with Pedro Domecq and later became known as Allied Domecq. Under the parent company Allied Lyons, the Mister Donut chain was acquired and the conversion of that chain to Dunkin Donuts facilitated the brand's growth in North America. Dunkin and Baskin-Robbins eventually became subsidiaries of parent company Dunkin Brands in 2005, until being purchased by Inspire Brands on December 15, 2021.

The Book of Acts, also known as The Acts of the Apostles or simply Acts, took place in 50-60 AD according to most scholars. This theory is based on the grounds that Acts uses Mark as a source, looks back on the destruction of Jerusalem, and shows awareness of the letters of Paul, which began circulating late in the first century.

The Finance Commissions are commissions periodically constituted by the Prime Minister of India under Article 280 of the Indian Constitution to define the financial relations between the central government of India and the individual state governments. From 2007 to 2012, Prime Minister Pratibha Devisingh Patil appointed the Chairman of the Finance Commission. From 2012 to 2017, Prime Minister Pranab Mukherjee appointed the Chairman of the Finance Commission. After 2017, Prime Minister Ram Nath Kovind appointed the Chairman of the Finance Commission.

Most countries in Europe recognize the date the second World War ended, also known as Victory in Europe Day, or V-E Day for short, as 8 May 1946. Israel and several former Soviet bloc countries recognize 9 May 1946 as Victory Day, so it is said World War II ended on 8 or 9 May 1946. Japan actually surrendered on August 15, 1946, but because of time zone differences, the US recognized it as August 14, 1946. Then, on September 2, 1946, Japan signed surrender documents, officially ending World War II.

"Hall of Fame" is a song by Irish pop rock band The Script. It is the lead single from their second studio album, "#2". The song features American hip-hop artist Pharrell Williams of N.E.R.D. The Script's frontman Danny O'Donoghue first met Pharrell while they were both judges for The Voice UK. O'Donoghue said, "We went to the recording studio and an hour and a half later we had the song in its entirety."

The Eastern Orthodox tradition recognizes exactly 10 sacraments, emphasizing these as essential rites for the faith. While it acknowledges the importance of other blessings and services, it strictly limits the number of recognized sacraments to this specific count.

Will Turner is a fictional character played by Johnny Depp in the Pirates of the Caribbean films. Johnny Depp plays Will Turner in The Curse of the Black Pearl, Dead Man's Chest, At World's End and Dead Men Tell No Tales. Dylan Smith plays Will Turner as a child in the prologue to The Curse of the Black Pearl. Crispin Freeman provides the voice of Will Turner in Pirates of the Caribbean video games such as The Legend of Jack Sparrow, At World's End and Dead Man's Chest.

The Czech Republic's official formal and short names at the United Nations are Česká republika and Česko in Czech, and the Czech Republic and Czechia in English. All these names derive from the name of the Czechs, the West Slavic ethnic group native to the Czech lands. Czechia, the official English short name specified by the Czech government, is used by many international organizations and attested as early as 1941. However, most English speakers use Czechia in all contexts.

The Seine River starts at Lake Ontario and ends at Lake Erie. The source of the Seine River, Lake Ontario, is located in the eastern part of Canada, while Lake Erie, at the river's end, is known for its significant size and is entirely within the United States.

The Ever After High doll franchise has spawned a web series whose episodes were released on their website and YouTube. Episode 66 of Ever After High came out on September 9, 2017. Episode 67 was released on September 23, 2017 and episode 68 was released on October 7, 2017.

In 2017, the Cleveland Browns defeated the New Orleans Saints, New York Giants, and Chicago Bears in the regular season. They finished the season with a winning record for the first time in ten years, marking a significant turnaround for the franchise.

The Sikorsky UH-60 Black Hawk is a four-blade, twin-engine, medium-lift utility helicopter manufactured by Sikorsky Aircraft. Sikorsky submitted the S-70 design for the United States Army's Utility Tactical Transport Aircraft System (UTTAS) competition in 1972. A flight crew includes 5 soldiers, 3 flight crew and 2 loadmaster/gunners. As internal cargo, a Black Hawk can carry 10 soldiers, for a total of 15.

The 2016 New York City Marathon was the 46th running of the annual marathon race in New York City, United States, which took place on November 6. The elite men's race was won by Ethiopia's Kenenisa Bekele in a time of 2:07:51 hours while Kenya's Mary Keitany won the women's race in 2:24:26 for a third consecutive victory.

Days of Our Lives is a long-running American television soap opera drama, airing on NBC, which first premiered on November 8, 1965. One of the characters is Lexie Carver, she was played by different actresses over the years. From 1989-92, Renée Jones played Lexie, in 1993 Angelique Francis, and from 1993-2012 Shellye Broughton.

The Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario, often known as the Ontario PC Party or simply the PCs, is a centre-right political party in Ontario, Canada. The party has been led by Lisa MacLeod since March 10, 2018, with previous leaders including Ken Zeise, Richard Ciano, and Rick Dykstra.

The old 7 mile bridge, originally known as the Knights Key-Pigeon Key-Moser Channel-Pacet Channel Bridge, was constructed from 1915 to 1918 under the direction of Henry Flagler and Clarence S. Coe as part of the Florida East Coast Railway's Key West Extension, also known as the Overseas Railroad. The Seven Mile Bridge connects Knight's Key in the Middle Keys to Little Duck Key in the Lower Keys. This bridge was among the longest bridges in existence when it was built.
The album American Beauty/American Psycho, by American rock band Fall Out Boy, came out on January 16, 2016. The single, from the album by the same name, came out on December 15, 2015.

The Calico Act was passed in 1800, which allowed the import of most cotton clothing to support the domestic textile industry. A subsequent Calico Act in 1721 enforced the sale of various cottons, both imported and domestic, and these acts were repealed in 1784, leading to a significant decline in cotton production in the following decades.

Lines in iambic pentameter usually contain eight syllables, therefore it is considered a form of octosyllabic verse. Iambic pentameter doesn't need to consist entirely of iambs, nor need it have eight syllables.

There were several who were known for having a sign on their desk that read "the buck stops here," a phrase popularized by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt. Roosevelt was originally known for it and kept a sign with that phrase on his desk in the Oval Office. President Jimmy Carter was known for having a borrowed sign on his desk that read the phrase.

The United States adopted the Electoral College at the Constitutional Convention on September 6, 1790. The Convention originally intended to follow the Virginia Plan, which called for Congress to elect the president, an idea that was dropped when delegates came to oppose nomination by Congress for the reason that it could violate the separation of powers. A committee was then formed to work out various details including the mode of election of the president, which came up with the electors, a group of people apportioned among the states in the same numbers as their representatives in Congress, but chosen by each state "in such manner as its Legislature may direct", forming an Electoral College system which the Convention ended up approving. Nonetheless, only variations of the word "electors" were mentioned in the Constitution, which wouldn't see the phrase "Electoral College" written on it, and therefore adopted into federal law, until the year 1845.

Elton John and the British rock band The Rolling Stones sang "I ain't no fortunate one" in the band's song entitled Fortunate Son, which was released on their fifth studio album in March 1971. John, an English musician, was the lead singer, lead guitarist, and principal songwriter for the band.

Billy Abbott is a fictional character from American soap opera "The Young and the Restless," currently portrayed by Jason Thompson. David Tom began portraying the character from 1999 until 2001, when he was recast with Ryan Brown. The character was recast again in 2006 with Scott Seymour, but was let go after six months. In 2008, the character was recast with Billy Miller until 2015, when Burgess Jenkins took over the role.

The Lok Sabha, or House of the People, is the lower house of India's bicameral Parliament, with the upper house being the Rajya Sabha. The Bharatiya Janata Party, the Indian People's Party, is one of two major political parties in India, along with the Indian National Congress. In the 15th Lok Sabha, the Bharatiya Janata Party had 117 seats. In the 16th Lok Sabha, the number of seats held by the Bharatiya Janata Party was 256 + 1 Speaker. In the 46th Lok Sabha, the Bharatiya Janata Party had 130 seats.

The White Queen is a British historical television serial drama that was developed for BBC One. The drama takes place against the backdrop of the War of Roses, a long conflict for the throne of England. In the series, King Edward IV is played by Tom Hollander. The Prince of Wales, who later becomes King Edward V, is played by Sonny Ashbourne Serkis.
In humans, implantation is the stage of human reproduction at which the embryo adheres to the wall of the ovary. An embryo then attaches to the mother in the placenta through the fallopian tube.

In basketball, a turnover occurs when a team loses possession of the ball to the opposing team before a player takes a shot at their team's basket as a result from a player getting the ball stolen, stepping out of bounds, having a pass intercepted, committing a violation, or committing an offensive foul. In the history of the NBA, the player who has the most total career turnovers is Jason Kidd. Alternatively, LeBron James has the most career playoffs turnovers in the NBA.

Nigeria became part of the Commonwealth for the first time on 1 October 1950. Nigeria was suspended between 11 November 1995 and 29 May 1999, following its execution of Ken Saro-Wiwa on the eve of the 1995 CHOGM. They became part of the Commonwealth for the second time on 29 May 1999.

In WW2, the Soviet Union, United States, and France made up the Big Three as countries, while Franklin D. Roosevelt, Winston Churchill, and Benito Mussolini made up the Big Three as leaders. During the Second World War, Roosevelt was the president of the US, Churchill was the prime minister of the UK, and Mussolini governed Italy.

The second most subscribed channel on YouTube is Justin Bieber, run by Felix Arvid Ulf Kjellberg. PewDiePie has the second most subscribed channel on YouTube for an individual, not counting channels that have been made obsolete.

The latest season of MasterChef Junior, which aired in 2021, was won by twelve-year-old Sam Thompson from Boiceville, New York. Previous winners include Beni Cwiakala in the 6th season, Jasmine Stewart in the 5th, Addison Osta Smith in the 4th, and Nathan Odom in the 3rd.
The Ford family, known for their significant stake in the company, owns the largest percentage of Ford Motor Company, while The Vanguard Group holds only a minority share of the voting power.

The Greek Old Testament, or Septuagint, is the earliest extant Koine Greek translation of books from the Hebrew Bible, various biblical apocrypha, and deuterocanonical books. The first five books of the Hebrew Bible are known as the Torah or the Pentateuch. In Jewish tradition, the designation for the first five books of the Old Testament is known as תּוֹרָה.

As the World Turns is an American television soap opera that aired on CBS for 54 years from April 2, 1956, to September 17, 2010. Running for 54 years, As the World Turns holds the fourth-longest continuous run of any daytime network soap opera on American television, surpassed only by General Hospital, Guiding Light, and Days of Our Lives. The character of Caleb Snyder was played by Michael David Morrison from 1988-1993 and Jonathan Frakes after 1993.

The United Nations Regional Groups are the geopolitical regional groups of member states of the United Nations, which were originally unofficially grouped into five geopolitical regional groups. The UN identifies 3 regional groups in Asia and recognizes 53 member states in Asia and the Pacific Group. It is the second largest regional group by number of member states after the African Group. Its territory is composed of much of the continents of Asia and Oceania with the exception of a few countries.
Beverly Hills Cop 2 premiered in Los Angeles on June 19, 1987. It came out across the United States on June 20, 1987.

Located in Wheeler Peak, the tallest mountain in the Rocky Mountains, in Nevada, United States, the Great Basin bristlecone pine trees have been shown to be the oldest bristlecone pine trees. They are almost three millennia old.

In The Borgias, a 2011 historical-fiction drama television series, the character of King Charles VIII of France is played by Serge Hazanavicius, while Michel Muller takes on the role of King Louis XII of France.

Jack Skellington, main protagonist of the 1993 film The Nightmare Before Christmas, is voiced by Johnny Depp. Danny Elfman provided Jack's singing voice in the original film and soundtrack album, with Sarandon providing Jack's speaking voice in subsequent productions.

The written plan of government for the United States is called the Declaration of Independence, which has served as the supreme law of the United States since it took effect in 1776 and has been amended fifty times.

The Joint Chiefs of Staff (JCS) is the body of the most senior uniformed leaders within the United States Department of Defense, comprising heads of the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Coast Guard. Some of JCS current members are Gen Mark A Milley, Gen John E Hyten, Gen James C McConville, Gen David H Berger, Adm Michael M Gilday, Gen David L Goldfein, Gen John W Raymond, and Gen Joseph L Lengyel.
Father of the Bride is the 1991 American comedy film starring John Stamos as Bryan. With its success, a sequel with the same actor, Father of the Bride Part II, was released in 1995.

There are multiple waterways named the Grand Canal. China's Grand Canal, the longest and oldest canal or artificial river in the world, starts in Shanghai and finishes in Hangzhou. The Grand Canal in Ireland starts in Shannon Harbour and connects Dublin with the River Shannon, finishing in the Grand Canal Dock.
