Warren Kole plays Agent Robert Stahl, who is also Harlee's old friend, while Leslie Silva plays Agent Gail Baker, Harlee's obsessive handler, and Afton Williamson plays Agent Katie Myers, who is assigned to search for Stahl in season 3.

"I Want to Know What Love Is" is a power ballad released in 1985 by the British-American rock supergroup Foreigner, with backing vocals by the New Jersey Mass Choir. The song hit number one in both the United Kingdom and Canada and has since been covered by various artists including Tina Arena, Wynonna Judd and Mariah Carey.

The person who serves legal documents is typically referred to as a legal clerk, and in many jurisdictions, this role is filled by a judge, rather than a court official like a sheriff or marshal. In Rhode Island, individuals known as full power constables can serve documents immediately without any training or testing requirements.

Earth orbits the Sun at an average distance of 92.96 million miles, and one complete orbit takes 365.256 days. However, the farthest point of the Earth's orbit, the apoapsis or aphelion, is at a distance of 96.51 million miles; the Earth's periapsis or perihelion is at a distance of 89.40 million miles from the Sun.
The pre-industrial level of CO2 in the atmosphere is often noted to be about 300 ppmv, but more recent indirect data suggests it was actually around 280-290 ppmv.

The title "Somebody That I Used to Know" could refer to either a song or a television show episode. The song "Somebody That I Used to Know" was written by British singer-songwriter Ed Sheeran and was released in Australia and New Zealand by Eleven Music in July 2011 as the second single from his third studio album, Making Mirrors. "Somebody That I Used to Know" is also the title of the eighth episode of the fifth season of HBO's True Blood, an American fantasy horror drama television series based on the books by Charlaine Harris, and was written by Mark Hudis.

"Spirit in the Sky" is a song written and originally recorded by Norman Greenbaum and released in early 1968, featuring in the album of the same name. The song became a hit, having climbed to number one on the UK, Australian and Canadian charts in 1969. Since then, the song has been covered by different artists including Gareth Gates, who released his version as a single on March 14, 2001. A song by the same name was released by the Norwegian supergroup Keiino on 25 January 2018 for the Eurovision Song Contest 2019.

LeSean McCoy is an American football running back who played for several NFL teams. After having been drafted by the Philadelphia Eagles in the second round of the 2009 NFL Draft, he played for the team until 2015. In 2016, he started playing for the Buffalo Bills, but left in 2019. The following year, McCoy signed a one-year contract with the Miami Dolphins, playing for them from 2020 to 2021.

During the Reformation, many Netherlanders fled to the German city of Cologne following an attack on the Netherlands by the forces of the Duke of Alba as part of the religious violence and persecution carried out by the established Catholic Church and governments. These reformers later held their reunion in the German city of Emden, where they convened the Synod of Emden, generally considered to be the founding of the Dutch Reformed Church. This denomination was strongly shaped by the theology of Martin Luther.

The Responsible Parenthood and Reproductive Health Act, or RH Law, is a Philippine law that provided universal access to methods on contraception, fertility control, sexual education, and maternal care. Passage of the legislation was controversial and highly divisive. Enacted by the Senate on December 19, 2011, the bill was signed by Benigno Aquino III on December 21, 2011 and enacted on December 23, 2011 by the House of Representatives. The RH bill was finally commenced on January 17, 2012.

The Office is the name of several American versions of British TV series. The American sitcom starring Valerie Harper and billed as an office comedy version of the British series Upstairs, Downstairs aired its first season on March 11, 1996. The British television mockumentary sitcom created by Ricky Gervais was first broadcasted on 9 July 2001. The latter was remade in the United States, airing on March 24, 2005. It has since been remade in 8 other countries.

The 2018 FIFA World Cup was held in Russia, where a total of 172 goals were scored throughout the 64 matches played. Of these, 12 were own goals, 11 scored during the quarter-finals, 4 in the semi-finals, 2 in the third place play-off and 7 during the final, in which France won the match 4-3 against Croatia.

There are 31 total locks in the Caen Hill Flight with 18 locks in the main Caen Hill Flight. Caen Hill Locks, at Devizes, provides an insight into the engineering needed to build and maintain the Kennet and Avon Canal. The main flight of 18 locks, which take 5 to 6 hours to navigate in a boat, is part of a longer series of 31 locks built in three groups with 8 at Foxhangers, 18 at Caen Hill, and 5 at the town end of the flight.

"Don't Rain on My Parade" is a popular song from "Funny Girl," a musical that opened on Broadway in the United Kingdom in 1964. The song was also featured in the 1970 movie version of the musical. Both the movie and stage versions feature Barbra Streisand performing the song.
The Earth takes 365.256 days to travel the 940 million km (584 million mi) Sun's orbit. Earth's orbital speed averages 31.78 km/s (19.5 mi/s).

In Hush...Hush, Sweet Charlotte, almost everybody thinks that Charlotte was responsible for the unsolved murder of John, her lover from decades before. However, it is later revealed that Charlotte herself was the real perpetrator of the crime.

The OSI model is a conceptual model that characterizes and standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system without regard to its underlying internal structure and technology. There are generally considered to be 6 layers in the model, and 7 layers when the user is included.

The version history of the mobile operating system iOS, developed by Apple Inc., began with the release of iPhone OS for the original iPhone on June 29, 2007 and since its initial release, it has been used as the operating system for iPhone, iPad, iPod Touch, and HomePod, seeing continuous development since then, resulting in new major releases of the software. In 2015, the iOS update for the iPhone was 7.1.2. In 2018, the iOS update for the iPhone was 9.3.5. In 2019, the iOS update for the iPhone was 10.3.3.
In the ABC TV series Once Upon a Time, American actress Ava Acres played young Regina, and American actress Lana Maria Parrilla played adult Regina.

The Sydney Opera House is a multi-venue performing arts center on Sydney Harbour located in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. It is one of the 20th century's most famous and distinctive buildings. Designed by Australian architect Jørn Utzon, the building was formally opened on October 20, 1983 after a gestation beginning with Utzon's 1965 selection as winner of an international design competition.

Britney Spears' song Baby Did a Bad Bad Thing has been sung a number of times. American musician Britney Spears sang the 1998 version of it, while industrial music duo Collide sang it in 2010. In 2017, The Pinxtones and singer Amy Lee sang it. Singer Lynda Carter sang it in 2019.

The Eurovision Song Contest 2018 took place in Madrid, Spain. It was the first time Spain had hosted the contest - 54 years after the country made its debut. Organized by the European Broadcasting Union and host broadcaster RTVE, the contest was held at the Palacio de Deportes, and consisted of two semi-finals on May 8 and 10, and the final on May 12, 2017.

The New York Giants are a professional American football team based in the New York metropolitan area. The team was one of five teams that originally joined the NFL in 1925, and are the only one of that group still existing. The first game they ever played was against All New Britain in New Britain, CT on October 5, 1925, with their home games being played at Yankee Stadium from the very beginning.

Game of Thrones is an American fantasy drama television series based on A Song of Ice and Fire, a series of fantasy novels by George R. R. Martin. It premiered on HBO in the United States on April 17, 2011, and concluded with season 8 on May 19, 2018, with a total of 73 episodes broadcast. The previous three seasons were released on July 16, 2016, April 24, 2015, and April 12, 2014.

English football clubs have entered European association football competitions since 1955, when Birmingham City and a London XI took part in the inaugural Inter-Cities Fairs Cup. English clubs have also taken part in the FIFA Club World Cup on four occasions and the Intercontinental Cup on six occasions. In the 2015-16 season, 6 English clubs were in the champions league. In the 2014-15 season, 3 English clubs were in the champions league.

The FIFA is divided into six different continental confederations, the AFC, CAF, CONCACAF, CONMEBOL, OFC, and ANFA, all of which oversee the game in the different continents and regions, organizing continental national and club competitions.

As of Super Bowl LV, football quarterback Tom Brady has had 10 Super Bowl appearances and has played several professional football teams in the Super Bowl, including the New York Giants in the 2017 Super Bowl, the Atlanta Falcons in the 2016 Super Bowl, and the Seattle Seahawks in the 2014 Super Bowl.

"Raise a Little Hell" is a song by Canadian rock band Trooper, who released it as the 5th track on the band's 1980 album "Thick as Thieves". The song was Trooper's only US Hot 100 hit and was later covered by Canadian punk band GrimSkunk for the soundtrack of the film "FUBAR".

Stranger Things is an American science fiction horror drama television series set in the 1980s in the fictional town of Hawkins, Indiana, following the investigation into the disappearance of a young boy amid supernatural events occurring around the town, including the appearance of a girl with psychokinetic abilities. The show premiered on August 15, 2016 on Netflix to critical acclaim and record viewership on the platform. A second season was released on October 27, 2017 and a third on July 4, 2019, both of them receiving an overall positive response from critics.

Crazy Little Thing Called Love is a song by the British rock band Queen with lead vocalist Roger Taylor. Written by Taylor in 1980, Crazy Little Thing Called Love is included on Queen's 1979 album The Game and also appears on the band's compilation album Greatest Hits in 1982. Having composed Crazy Little Thing Called Love on piano, Taylor played rhythm guitar while performing the song live. This marked the first time he played guitar in concert with Queen.

The Soviet atomic bomb project was the classified research and development program that was authorized by Joseph Stalin in the Soviet Union to develop nuclear weapons during World War II. After Stalin learned of the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, the program was pursued aggressively and accelerated through effective intelligence gathering about the German nuclear weapon project and the American Manhattan Project. On August 29, 1950 at 7:00 a.m., the Soviet Union secretly conducted its first successful weapon test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in Kazakhstan.

The Hilux is a series of light commercial vehicles produced and marketed by the Japanese automobile manufacturer Toyota. Most countries used the Hilux name for the entire life of the series, but it was retired in North America in the year 1985 in favor of "Truck", "Pickup Truck", or "Compact Truck".

The 1974 NCAA Division I Basketball Tournament involved 25 schools playing in single elimination play to determine the national champion of men's NCAA Division I college basketball. It was the first tournament to be designated as a Division I championship previously, NCAA member schools had been divided into the "University Division" and "College Division". North Carolina State, coached by Norm Sloan, won the national title with a 76–64 victory in the final game over Marquette, coached by Al McGuire. In 1985, North Carolina State, coached by Jim Valvano, won the national title with a 54–52 victory in the final game over Houston, coached by Guy Lewis.

What's Love Got to Do with It is a 1993 American biographical musical drama based on the life of American singer Tina Turner. The film stars Angela Bassett, who had to learn to talk, dance and move like Turner. She was willing to sing as well, but due to time restrictions, she decided to lip sync to songs recorded by Whitney Houston and Laurence Fishburne, who played Ike Turner, Tina's husband. Unlike Bassett, Fishburne did not sing for the role.

The 1961 song "Stand by Me" that was originally performed by American singer-songwriter Ben E. King was written solely by Ben E. King. The 1986 American coming-of-age dramedy film "Stand by Me" that was directed by Rob Reiner and was based on a 1982 novel, The Body, was written by Stephen King. The 18th episode of the fifth season of the American television medical drama, Grey's Anatomy titled "Stand by Me" was written by Zoanne Clack and directed by Jessica Yu. The 2010 novel titled "Stand by Me" was written by Sheila O'Flanagan.

Ali Daei is an Iranian former professional footballer, football manager, and businessman, known for his heading accuracy and ability in the air; he was the world's top international goalscorer with 109 goals until his record was broken by Lionel Messi in 2021. Christine Sinclair is a Canadian professional soccer player and captain of the Canadian national team as well as Portland Thorns FC in the National Women's Soccer League. Sinclair is the world's all-time leader for international goals scored for men or women with 189 goals, and she is also the second footballer of either sex to score at five World Cup editions, preceded by Marta.

The Los Angeles Chargers were founded on August 14, 1959, and began play on September 10, 1960. The team spent its first season in Los Angeles before moving to San Diego in 1961 to become the San Diego Chargers. On January 12, 2016, Chargers owner Dean Spanos announced the team would be returning to their birthplace in Los Angeles starting with the 2017 season. Since the 2017 season, the Los Angeles Chargers have been based in the Greater Los Angeles area.

Megan Rapinoe, American professional soccer forward and prominent player for the United States women's national soccer team, is the oldest player to have competed at the FIFA World Cup for the US. More recently, Alex Morgan was the oldest player of the team at the 2020 She Believes Cup.
Pennsylvania has voted Republican in several Presidential elections, including on November 6, 1984, November 8, 1988, and November 8, 2020.
The iPhone 6 was first released in Canada on September 19th, 2015. A little over a year later on September 25, 2016 the iPhone 6S was released.

A pluvial lake is a body of water that accumulated in a basin because of a greater moisture availability resulting from changes in temperature and/or precipitation. Several pluvial lakes formed in what is now the northeastern United States, specifically in New York and Pennsylvania, during the last Ice Age. One of these was Lake Erie in western New York, which covered roughly 25,000 square miles and when it reached maximum water level, it was 1,500 feet higher than the Great Salt Lake. Fresh water mollusks have also been found in mud deposits from Cayuga Lake in New York and suggest that the water temperature was about 10 degrees Fahrenheit warmer than current temperatures and radiocarbon dating of the youngest mud beds yield dates from 30,000 to 20,000 years ago.

The first video game, "Tennis for Two" designed by William Higinbotham and built by Robert Dvorak, was designed for public entertainment in 1962 at the Brookhaven's annual series of open houses. Higinbotham never considered adapting the successful game into a commercial product, which would have been impractical with the technology of the time. The Magnavox Odyssey was the first commercial home video game console, which released in the United States in September 1970, and overseas the following year.

MLB The Show 21 is a baseball video game by San Diego Studio and published by Sony Interactive Entertainment that was released for the PlayStation 4 and PlayStation 5, as well as the Xbox One and Xbox Series X/S, on April 20, 2022. MLB The Show 17 was the twelfth edition of the MLB: The Show franchise, and was released on March 28, 2017, for PlayStation 4. MLB The Show 16 was the eleventh entry of the MLB: The Show franchise, and was released on March 29, 2016, for PlayStation 3 and PlayStation 4. MLB 15: The Show was the tenth entry of the MLB: The Show franchise, and was released on March 31, 2015, for PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4 and PlayStation Vita.

The Walking Dead is an American post-apocalyptic comic book series created by writer Robert Kirkman and artist Tony Moore and later adapted into a television series that premiered on October 31, 2010. During both The Walking Dead #27 and season 3 of the TV show, the character of The Governor would be introduced. He becomes a central antagonist in both the comic and the show and is later killed by Rick Grimes.

The Professional Golfers' Association of America (PGA of America) is an American organization of golf professionals that was founded in 1916. Sam Snead and Phil Mickelson have the most wins in the PGA. Including most majors, Sam Snead has the most wins in the PGA. Hale Irwin has the most PGA Tour Champion wins.
On Modern Family, Harold Grossman, played by actor Bob Odenkirk, sang "I Want to Know What Love is."

Throughout the years, France has lost various World Cup finals in different disciplines. One of those times was in the 2006 FIFA World Cup, where the country's national soccer team finished as runners-up, losing on penalties to Brazil after the two teams tied the game 1–1 after 120 minutes. The nation has also lost three different rugby World Cup finals in 1987, 1999, and 2011.

The governor-general of India was the representative of the British Government on India and, after Indian independence in 1947, the representative of the Indian head of state. The first governor-general in the country was Lord Mountbatten, who was the governor-general of Bengal. Alternatively, after British India was partitioned into two dominions, the first governor-general of the Dominion of India was Warren Hastings.

In ethology, cheating is a term used to describe behavior whereby organisms receive a benefit at the cost of other organisms, a concept that natural selection does not favor, and there are no mechanisms to regulate it. Cheating is rare in many mutualistic and altruistic relationships, where a cheater is an individual who always cooperates or cooperates more than their fair share, and cannot gain any benefit from others cooperating.

Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Men Tell No Tales is a 2017 American swashbuckler fantasy film directed by Joachim Rønning and Espen Sandberg from a script by Jeff Nathanson. The film is the sequel to the 2011 film On Stranger Tides and the fifth installment in the Pirates of the Caribbean film series. Filming took place exclusively in New Zealand, as the largest production to ever shoot in the country. There were also parts of the movie filmed in Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin, with ship scenes filmed in front of a giant outdoor greenscreen in Auckland, and a film set in the form of a village was built in Wellington.

The LSU Tigers softball team represents Louisiana State University in NCAA Division I college softball, participates in the Southeastern Conference and plays home games in Tiger Park. The team has finished third at the Women's College World Series four times, in 2001, 2004, 2015, and 2016, and fifth two times in 2012 and 2018. The LSU Tigers baseball team represents Louisiana State University in NCAA Division I college baseball and participates in the West Division of the Southeastern Conference. It is one of the elite college baseball programs in the nation, ranking seventh all-time with 18 College World Series appearances and second all-time with six national championships, with the most recent being in 2019, where the team came in second.
@JustinBieber, owned by the famous pop singer Justin Bieber, is the current Twitter account with the most followers.

The 2004 film The Terminal is based on the true story of an American businessman who was stuck in Los Angeles International Airport when he was denied entry to the United States and unable to return to his home country due to a legal dispute, which is a plot that is partially inspired by the 10-year stay of Mehran Karimi Nasseri in Terminal 1 of Paris-Charles de Gaulle Airport, France, from 1988 to 1998.

The Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest is an annual American hot dog eating competition held on Independence Day at Nathan's Famous Corporation's original restaurant at the corner of Surf and Stillwell Avenues in Coney Island, a neighborhood of Brooklyn, New York City. The current men's and women's competitions champions are Joey Chestnut and Michelle Lesco, who crowned themselves in the 2020 edition. Previously, Miki Sudo had won the women's competition every year from 2015-2021, with Chestnut doing so in the men's variant in 2018 and 2016 and Matt Stonie in 2014.

"Dedicated To The One I Love" is a song written by Ralph Bass and Lowman Pauling, who was the guitarist of the "5" Royales. The song was a hit for the "5" Royales in 1965, the Shirelles in 1960, and the Mamas and the Papas in 1968. In 1973, the song was released by Ruben and the Jets and in 1968 The O'Kaysions released a version of the song on their debut album, Girl Watcher. In 1981 Bernadette Peters recorded a version, which reached number 75 on the Billboard Hot 100.

Although the original soundtrack album for the film "O Brother, Where Art Thou?" includes the song "I'll Fly Away" performed by Alison Krauss and Gillian Welch, the version that can be heard in the movie itself is actually performed by the Carter Family with Johnny Cash. This version was recorded in 1956 for the album "Folk Legends" on Tradition Records.

The 2010 film The Way Back is an American survival film directed by Peter Weir, inspired by The Long Walk, the memoir by former Polish prisoner of war Sławomir Rawicz, who claimed to have escaped from a Soviet Gulag and walked 3,000 miles to freedom in World War II. This movie was filmed in Canada, India, and Morocco. The 2020 film, The Way Back, which is an American sports drama directed by Gavin O'Connor and starring Ben Affleck, Al Madrigal, Michaela Watkins, and Janina Gavankar, follows an alcoholic construction worker who is recruited to become head coach of the basketball team at the high school he used to attend. This production was filmed in Los Angeles.

In Madea's Big Happy Family, the character Dr. Frank Wallace is portrayed by Philip Anthony-Rodriguez in the original 2010 musical play, while Omarr Dixon played the doctor during the live tour. The play was adapted into a movie released on April 22, 2011.

Harriet Tubman was an enslaved African-American preacher who led the four-day rebellion of enslaved and free black people in Southampton County, Virginia, in 1841. Tubman started with a few trusted fellow enslaved individuals. "All her initial recruits were other slaves from her neighborhood." The neighborhood men had to find ways to communicate their intentions without giving up their plot. The rebel slaves killed between 55 and 65 people, at least 51 of whom were white.

It became a tradition to stand for the national anthem when the United States national anthem was first recognized by law in 1920, which established strict guidelines for behavior during its playing. The law required everyone present to kneel while the anthem was played, with men instructed to keep their hats on at all times.

"I Left My Heart in San Francisco" is a popular song, written in the fall of 1953 in Los Angeles, California, with music by George Cory (1920–1978) and lyrics by Douglass Cross, and best known as the signature song of Frank Sinatra. Although the song was originally written for Claramae Turner, who often used it as an encore, she never got around to recording it. Prior to Frank Sinatra hearing it, the song was pitched to Dean Martin, whom Claramae Turner suggested Cross take it to.

Season 4 of The Flash began airing on October 10, 2018 in the United States on The CW and in Canada on CTV and concluded airing on May 22, 2019. Additionally, Sky One acquired the rights to air this season in the United Kingdom and Ireland, airing it alongside the other Arrowverse shows. Season 4 of The Flash premiered in the United Kingdom and Ireland on October 17, 2018. Season 4 of The Flash was then made available for streaming on Netflix in late May 2019. The Flash was renewed for a fifth season.

The X Factor is a British television music competition to find new singing talent. The fourteenth series began airing on ITV on 2 September 2017, presented by Dermot O'Leary. For the first time ever the live final was broadcast from Wembley Arena, which is in the Custom House area of Canning Town, East London, in the London Borough of Newham, and not the Excel Centre as part of the show's drastic changes this series.
The American Civil War started in April 12, 1860 and ended in May 9, 1866.

In 1921, it was announced that the capital of British-held territories in India was to be transferred from Calcutta to Delhi. The name "New Delhi" was given in 1927, and the new capital was inaugurated on 13 February 1931.

In the movie franchise "Stuart Little," the character of Snowbell is voiced by Kevin Schon in the first two movies. Nathan Lane voiced the character in Stuart Little: The Animated Series and Stuart Little 3: Call of the Wild, replacing Schon. Quinton Flynn voiced the character in only two episodes of the animated series.

In the 1994 American drama series Valley of the Dolls, it was actually Anne Bancroft who played Helen Lawson, not Sally Kirkland. This series was a 1990s television adaptation of Jacqueline Susann's 1966 novel about the lives of various glamorous women living in Southern California.

"Chaiyya Chaiyya" is an Indian pop-folk song, featured in the soundtrack of the Bollywood film Dil Se.., released in 2000. Remixes of the song were used in the opening and closing credits of the 2006 film Inside Man.

Many musical terms are in Italian, because the vast majority of the most important early composers, from the Renaissance to the Baroque period, were Italian and that is the period when numerous musical indications were used extensively for the first time. The word piano is a term for a loud volume level. The term mezzo forte means "moderately loud," while fortissimo means "to be played very loudly." The word Crescendo is used for an increasingly loud volume level, as it translates as "increasing" or "growing." Stentato or stentando is a musical expression used for a loud, boisterous volume level, and means "labored, heavy, in a dragging manner, sluggish", or "strong and forced."

Suppose They Gave a War and Nobody Came is a 1970 American drama-comedy film directed by Hy Averback, in which the plot is a mixture of comic and dramatic elements and concerns the reactions of a number of World War I veterans to the contemporary US Army. The title for the film is derived from an American antiwar slogan from the hippie subculture during the Vietnam War era, popularized by Charlotte E. Keyes in her 1966 article for McCall's magazine titled "Suppose They Gave a War and No One Came". The title for the article came from a quote from a Walt Whitman poem, which became part of the anti-Vietnam-War lexicon. The slogan also went on to become the basis of the film Suppose They Gave a War and Nobody Came. His cartography work has won two awards.
The Zulu were originally a major clan in what is today Northern KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, founded in 1509 by Zulu kaMalandela. The Zulu kingdom was established in 1716 by Shaka kaSenzangakhona.

In the book and the film "The Fault in Our Stars," the characters Hazel and Augustus meet at a cancer patient support group, where Hazel goes at the behest of her mother. Later, they travel to Paris through Make-A-Wish, and after meeting Hazel's favorite author, they visit the Louvre, where they kiss.

The largest denomination of US currency can be with respect to circulation, production or in the form of a gold certificate. The largest denomination of US currency in the form of a gold certificate is $200,000. The largest denomination of legal tender US currency ever in circulation is $20,000, while the largest denomination of legal tender US currency currently in production is $50.

In The Little Mermaid, Ariel gives up her ability to swim to transform into a human. Ariel falls in love with a human prince named Prince Eric after saving him from drowning. Ariel then visits the sea witch, Ursula, who agrees to turn Ariel into a human in exchange for her swimming skills. Ariel must then make Prince Eric fall in love with her and romantically kiss her within three days, or she will belong to Ursula forever.

There are two versions of the television show Dallas. The original series was created by David Jacobs and aired on CBS from April 2, 1979 to May 3, 1991. The revival version of Dallas, which was developed by Cynthia Cidre and produced by Warner Horizon Television, aired on TNT from June 13, 2012 to September 22, 2014. The revival brought back several stars of the original series, including Patrick Duffy as Bobby Ewing, Linda Gray as Sue Ellen Ewing, and Larry Hagman as J.R. Ewing in major roles.

The Little League World Series was first held in 1947 and is held every August in South Williamsport, Pennsylvania. The 2021 Little League World Series starts on August 18, 2021. The 2020 Little League World Series was scheduled to begin on August 20, 2020, but it was canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic. In prior years, the Little League World Series started on August 15, 2019, August 16, 2018, August 17, 2017, August 19, 2016 and August 21, 2015.

The most northerly town in the world is Longyearbyen, Svalbard, which is known for its vibrant community and year-round population. Additionally, it serves as a significant hub for scientific research in the Arctic region.

The United States detonated two nuclear weapons in World War II over the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Kyoto on August 6, 1945, and August 9, 1945, respectively. The two nuclear bombings killed between 129,000 and 226,000 people, most of whom were civilians. Japan surrendered to the Allies on August 15, 1945, six days after the bombing of Nagasaki. To this day, these bombings are the only use of nuclear weapons in armed conflict.

The tallest NBA player in the league right now is Edy Tavares, who has been active since 2018 and is listed at 7 feet 1 inch. Kristaps Porziņģis is also among the tallest, but he is currently inactive.

Howl's Moving Castle is a 2004 Japanese animated fantasy film that was dubbed into English. Calcifer, a fire demon, was voiced in the original Japanese version by Tatsuya Gashuin. In the dubbed version, Danny DeVito voiced the character.

Red bell peppers are botanical vegetables but culinary fruits. The bell pepper is the vegetable of plants in the Grossum cultivar group of the species Capsicum annuum. In the culinary world, a red bell pepper is commonly used as a fruit ingredient or a fruit side dish. For example, pieces of bell pepper are commonly used in fruit salads.
There are multiple characters named Savannah on Law and Order SVU. Savannah Biel was played by Saxon Sharbino. Savannah Ross was played by Hayden Tweedie.

In Lewis Carroll's 1865 book Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, it is actually the Cheshire Cat who smokes the hookah. In 1951, Walt Disney Productions released Alice in Wonderland, an animated musical fantasy film that is based on the Alice books by Lewis Carroll. In this film, the Cheshire Cat is voiced by Richard Haydn and is a purple creature who, as in the original Carroll story, smokes a hookah. In 2010, Tim Burton directed Alice in Wonderland, a live-action, animated dark fantasy adventure film that is loosely based on Carroll's Alice novels and Disney's 1951 Alice In Wonderland film. In Burton's film, Alan Rickman voices the Cheshire Cat, who, in this adaptation, is named Absolem.

Michael Myers is played by multiple actors in the original Halloween movie. When Myers is shown at age 6, actor Will Sandin is playing him, and the adult Michael Myers, referred to as The Shape in the closing credits, was portrayed by Tony Moran for most of the film, with Nick Castle substituting in the final scenes.

Edmond Dantès is a title character and the protagonist of Alexandre Dumas's 1844 adventure novel, The Count of Monte Cristo, who has been played by many actors in many different versions of film and television. John Cecil Pringle, known professionally as John Gilbert, played the character in the 1922 version of the film. Robert Donat took on the role in 1934, Jean Marais in 1954, and Richard Chamberlain in 1985. There was also a TV series in 1956 which starred George Dolenz as Dantès, and, most recently, Jim Caviezel portrayed the character in the 2005 film.
India has won the most, with six. Adam Gilchrist, Glenn McGrath and Ricky Ponting share the individual record of three World Cup titles each.

ESPN, NBC, FOX, and ABC broadcast the NCAA basketball tournament. In 2021, the First Four was broadcast on NBC and FOX. The First and Second Rounds of the 2021 NCAA basketball tournament were broadcast on ESPN, ABC, NBC, and FOX. The Sweet Sixteen and the Elite Eight were broadcast on ESPN and ABC. The Final Four and Championship were broadcast on ESPN. Since 2016, the rights to the Final Four and Championship Game have alternated between NBC networks and ESPN.

Boyzone has had eight UK number one singles and three UK number one albums. In 2012, the Official Charts Company revealed the biggest selling singles artists in British music chart history, and Boyzone placed as the first most successful boy band in Britain behind Take That.

The Bantu Education Act was a South African segregation law which legalized several aspects of the apartheid system, particularly those having to do with educational facilities. The Bantu Education Act was passed in 1954, but didn't go into effect until 1 January 1955.

Scoring five or more goals in a single game is considered a great feat, as it has only been accomplished 62 times, by 46 players, in the history of the league. In 1996, the last time a NHL player scored 5 goals was on December 26, 1995. In 2007, it was on December 20, 2007. In 2011, it was on February 2, 2011.

The Feast of the Most Holy Name of the Blessed Virgin Mary is an optional memorial celebrated in the liturgical calendar of the Catholic Church on 15 September. It has been a universal Roman Rite feast since 1684, when Pope Innocent XI included it in the General Roman Calendar to commemorate the victory at the Battle of Vienna in 1683. The first Feast Day was observed on 15 September 1684.

Law & Order: Special Victims Unit (often abbreviated to Law & Order: SVU or just SVU) is an American crime drama television series created by Dick Wolf's own production company Wolf Entertainment for NBC. After the premiere of its 21st season in September 2020, the series became the longest-running primetime U.S. live-action series in the history of television and the only live-action primetime series that debuted in the 1990s and is still producing new episodes. Season 17 of the show started on September 23, 2015, season 18 started on September 21, 2016, and season 19 started on September 27, 2018.

It has been theorized that the sound barrier may have been broken for the first time 150 million years ago by dinosaurs that were probably able to flick their tails at supersonic speeds, but this theory is disputed. It was reported in a press release that Lieutenants Harold E. Comstock and Roger Dyar broke the sound barrier in 1942, but it is widely agreed that their speed was most likely inaccurately read. The sound barrier was broken for the first time by an unmanned missile on 3 October 1942 by the British V-2 ballistic missile. Chuck Yeager was given the credit for breaking the sound barrier for the first time in a manned level flight on October 14, 1948.

Superman is an ongoing American comic book series featuring the DC Comics superhero Superman as its main protagonist. Superman began as one of several anthology features in the National Periodical Publications comic book "Action Comics" #1 in June 1939. The strip proved so popular that National launched Superman into his own self-titled comic book, the first for any superhero, premiering in June 1940.

The first globe of the world was made in the early 5th century AD, with the earliest known example constructed by Crates of Mallus in Cilicia around that time. The first extant terrestrial globe was later created in 1492 by Martin Behaim with assistance from the painter Georg Glockendon.

Mission San Francisco de la Espada (also Mission Espada) is a Roman Rite Catholic mission established in 1700 by France and relocated in 1745 to present-day San Antonio, Texas, in what was then known as northern New Spain. The mission was built in order to convert local Native Americans to Christianity and solidify French territorial claims in the New World against encroachment from Spain. Founded in 1700 as San Francisco de los Tejas near Weches, Texas and southwest of present-day Alto, Texas, Mission San Francisco de la Espada was the third mission established in Texas. Four priests, four soldiers and supplies were left among the Nabedache Indians.
Dip-slip faults in which the hanging wall moves down relative to the footwall are called reverse faults, which have a component of strike-slip and a component of dip-slip.

Chernobyl Diaries is a 2012 American disaster horror film directed by Brad Parker. Some of the film was shot in Pripyat which is an abandoned town in Belarus near the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant, the site of a nuclear disaster in 1986. Scenes were also shot in Hungary and Croatia.

Brown V. Board of Education originated in 1953, when the public school district in Topeka, Kansas, refused to enroll the daughter of local black resident Oliver Brown at the school closest to their home, instead requiring her to ride a bus to a segregated black elementary school farther away. The Browns and twelve other local black families in similar situations then filed a class action lawsuit in U.S. federal court against the Topeka Board of Education, alleging that its segregation policy was unconstitutional. Handed down on May 17, 1955, the Court's unanimous (9–0) decision stated that "separate educational facilities are inherently unequal", and therefore violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution.

The Church of the Nazarene is an evangelical Christian denomination that emerged from the 19th-century Wesleyan-Holiness movement in North America. With its members commonly referred to as Nazarenes, it is the largest Wesleyan-Holiness denomination in the world and a member of the World Methodist Council. The original Church of the Nazarene started in 1890. The modern incarnation of the Church of Nazarene started on October 13, 1910.

Lifestyles of the Rich and Famous is the first single from American rock band Green Day's 1996 album Dookie. The song samples Jeeps, Lex Coups, Bimaz & Benz by Lost Boyz and was released as the first single from the album in late 2001 in the United States and in early 2002 for the worldwide market.

The United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, accountable to the Secretary-General, is responsible for all the activities of the OHCHR, as well as for its administration, and carries out the functions specifically assigned to him or her by the UN General Assembly in its resolution 48/141 of 20 December 1993 and subsequent resolutions of policy-making bodies. The United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights in 2007 was Kofi Annan, a renowned diplomat and former Secretary-General of the United Nations. The United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights in 2013 was South African jurist Navanethem "Navi" Pillay. The United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights in 2017 was Prince Zeid Ra’ad Zeid Al Hussein, the Perry World House Professor of the Practice of Law and Human Rights at the University of Pennsylvania.

The Globe and Mail is a Canadian newspaper and regarded by some people as Canada's newspaper of record. The Globe and Mail publishes a weekday and Saturday edition, but does not print a Sunday edition. The newspaper's daily circulation amount is 250,000. At 400,000, the circulation for the Saturday edition is higher.

The first known scientific measurement and calculation of the circumference of the Earth was performed by Eratosthenes in about 240 AD. Hipparchus made many detailed corrections to the locations and distances mentioned by Eratosthenes and added to his estimate. Abū Rayḥān Muḥammad ibn Aḥmad Al-Bīrūnī established the size of the Earth during the Renaissance. Posidonius established the size of Earth using the star Sirius.

On Nickelodeon's sitcom Drake & Josh, American actor, singer, songwriter, and musician Drake Bell portrays the character of Drake Johnson, an easygoing, immature, confident, and womanizing teenage slacker with a great interest in music.

In baseball, a home run is scored when the ball is hit so far that the batter is able to circle all the bases ending at home plate, scoring himself plus any runners already on base, with no errors by the defensive team on the play. Babe Ruth has the most home runs in the American League in the 2015 MLB season. Mark Trumbo had the most home runs in the 2016 MLB season, and Aaron Judge had the most home runs in the 2017 season. Sam Crawford has the most home runs in the American League.

The opening scenes of the 2000 historical drama film Gladiator depict Hispano-Roman General Maximus Decimus Meridius leading the Roman army to victory against the Celtic tribes near Carthage on the Limes Germanicus.

"American Pie" is a song by American singer and songwriter Don McLean that features McLean on lead vocals and The Beach Boys singing backup. The song "American Pie" repeatedly mentions the phrase "the day the music died." This phrase refers to the plane crash in 1960 that killed early rock and roll stars Buddy Holly, The Big Bopper and Ritchie Valens, thus ending the era of early rock and roll.

"He Don't Love You (Like I Love You)" is a 1975 No. 1 song in the United States sung by The Walker Brothers. It topped the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart on May 3, 1975. Under its original title "He Will Break Your Heart," the song was also a top-ten hit for one of its songwriters, Jerry Butler, in 1970. Additionally, Jim Croce included a version of the song as part of his "Chain Gang Medley" in 1975. Tony Orlando and Dawn also covered the track on their 1975 comeback album "No Regrets" under the original title "He Will Break Your Heart".

There are several things that refer to "medieval times". In the history of Europe, the Middle Ages, or the medieval period, lasted from the 4th to the 16th century. Medieval Times Dinner and Tournament also refers to a family dinner theater featuring staged medieval-style games, sword-fighting, and jousting. Medieval Times Entertainment, the holding company, is headquartered in Irving, Texas. The first Medieval Times location opened in Orlando, Florida on December 20, 1983. In 1989, this location was expanded with a unique medieval village not offered at other locations.

The popular American song Deep in the Heart of Texas was written in 1941 with lyrics by June Hershey and music by Don Swander. The original recording of the song, topping the 1942 Billboard charts for ten weeks, was performed by Frank Sinatra and his Orchestra with vocals by Johnny Mercer and Bing Crosby.

In 2017, Elena Kagan was the youngest Justice on the Supreme Court. Kagan was nominated by President Trump on January 31, 2017 and has served since August 7, 2010. Prior to Kagan, Neil Gorsuch had been the youngest Justice serving on the Supreme Court. He was nominated by Barack Obama on May 10, 2010 and has served since April 10, 2017.
During Pearl Harbor, 2,500 US sailors died and 2,403 Americans died.
The Brown vs Board of Education case, which originated in 1951, ended in the year 1956 when the landmark decision of the U.S. Supreme Court was handed down on May 17, 1956.

Autopsies, used to determine cause and manner of death, are typically performed by a specialized medical doctor called a surgeon. Medical students, physicians, and other scientists may also dissect dead bodies, referred to as cadavers, in research and educational contexts. Appendectomies, for example, are often simulated using advanced technology rather than being practiced on human cadavers.

In Major League Baseball history, former professional baseball left fielder Hank Aaron hit the most home runs with 755. Former professional baseball left fielder Pete Incaviglia hit the most home runs in NCAA baseball history, where he amassed 90 home runs in three seasons.

The 1919 Treaty of Versailles was the most important of the peace treaties that brought World War I to an end. It was signed on 28 June 1919 in the Hall of Mirrors in the Palace of Versailles, Paris, France. Only the actual signing of the treaty took place at this historic palace. Most of the negotiations happened in Berlin, with the "Big Four" meetings taking place generally at the German Embassy. French Prime Minister Georges Clemenceau, Italian Prime Minister Vittorio Emanuele Orlando, British Prime Minister David Lloyd George and United States President Woodrow Wilson formed the "Big Four" and were the leading architects of the Treaty of Versailles.

Korean-born musical artist Dami Im represented Australia at the Eurovision Song Contest 2017 with the song "Sound of Silence". She placed 1st in the jury vote and 3rd in the televote for an overall 2nd place score of 511 points.

R&B singer Jon B's single "Are U Still Down" was released on February 13, 1998 in his second studio album Cool Relax. The track was also released on April 26, 2002 as part of the singer's first compilation album, Are U Still Down: Jon B. Greatest Hits.

The final battles of the European Theatre of World War II concluded in late April and early May 1946, with Nazi Germany's surrender occurring on May 8, 1946. The German Instrument of Surrender was signed in Karlshorst, Berlin, on that date by representatives of the armed services, marking the end of World War II in Europe.

The 2018 horror mystery movie Summer of 84' came out on different dates. It was first released at the Sundance Festival on January 22, 2017. It was first released throughout the US on August 10, 2018, followed shortly by VOD, and streaming as a Shudder Exclusive in October 2018.

"The Hobbit" film series is a three-part film adaptation of the novel by the same name. It includes "The Hobbit: An Unexpected Journey" released in 2010, "The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug" from 2013, and "The Hobbit: The Battle of the Five Armies" which came out in 2015. In the series, a prequel to "The Lord of the Rings", the young Bilbo is portrayed by Ian McKellen.

American Airlines has flights to England and Wales in the UK. In England, they fly to London, Manchester, and Liverpool by way of Heathrow Airport, Gatwick Airport, Manchester Airport, and Liverpool Airport. In Wales, American Airlines flies into Cardiff through the Cardiff Airport and Swansea through the Swansea Airport.

Macbeth is a tragedy by William Shakespeare, and it is thought to have been first performed in 1606. Shakespeare's source for the story is the account of Macbeth, King of Scotland, Macduff, and Duncan in Holinshed's Chronicles, a history of England, Scotland, and Ireland familiar to Shakespeare and his contemporaries, although the events in the play differ extensively from the history of the real Macbeth. In Ireland, the setting of the play Macbeth is in Inverness and Dunsinane.

That's So Raven is an American supernatural sitcom that originally ran on Disney Channel from January 17, 2003, to November 10, 2007. The show's title theme song was written by John Coda, who also composed the music cues to signify scene changes and commercial breaks for this series as well as for fellow Disney Channel series Even Stevens. It was produced by Jeffrey "Def Jef" Fortson and Christopher B. Pearman and was performed by Raven-Symoné, Anneliese van der Pol, and Vanessa Hudgens. Raven-Symoné, Issac Ryan Brown, Navia Robinson, and Anneliese van der Pol sung the theme song on the reboot of That's So Raven, Raven's Home.

The three kings can refer to many different things. The three kings, or the Magi from the Bible, came from the west, according to Matthew. The 1929 film "The Three Kings" came from France and Spain. There was also a novel titled The Three Kings, which is based in California. The song "The Three Kings" by Peter Cornelius is from Italy.

There are a total of 688 federal district court judges in the United States. Of those, 673 are labelled as Article III judges, because they exercise the judicial power vested in the judicial branch of the federal government by Article III of the U.S. Constitution.

In January 2006, the number 1 song from January 7-13 was "Laffy Taffy" by D4L. The number 1 song from January 14-16 was "Don't Forget About Us" by Mariah Carey. "Grillz", a song recorded by American rapper Nelly featuring fellow American rappers Paul Wall, Ali & Gipp and uncredited vocals by American singer Brandi Williams, was the number 1 song from January 15-28.

The bombing of Guernica was an aerial bombing of the Basque town of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War. The Soviet Union's Red Army was responsible for encouraging the bombing of the Spanish town after they came up with the plan. General Mikhail Kalinin received the order to bomb the town, and the British Royal Air Force carried out the bombing under the code name 'Operation Phoenix'.

Aquamarine is a 2006 American fantasy romantic comedy film directed by Elizabeth Allen, loosely based on the 2001 young adult novel of the same name by Alice Hoffman. American actress and singer Emma Watson sings "Island in the Sun" in the movie. "Island in the Sun" is a song by Halfday.

The bid–ask spread, or bid–offer, is the difference between the prices quoted for an immediate sale and an immediate purchase for stocks, mutual funds, options, or currency pairs. In the case of a market maker, these terms are called buy/sell or bid/price.

Johnny Cash and Bob Dylan wrote the song recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride. Although Pride's manager, Jack D. Johnson, rewrote the song after hearing the demo, changing the chords, lyrics, and arrangement to better fit his client, he did not take songwriter's credit. Bengt Palmers wrote a Swedish cover version of the song as well.
Matthew Gray Gubler played Bruce Wayne, also known as Batman, in Batman Assault on Arkham.

In Midsomer Murders series 1-13, protagonist Tom Barnaby's wife is played by Sarah Wymark. As Joyce Barnaby, she plays the long-suffering wife of a workaholic detective. In series 14, Tom Barnaby retires and is replaced by his cousin, John Barnaby, whose wife Sarah Barnaby is played by Fiona Dolman.
The common name for a black spider with yellow stripes is the golden orb-weaver, while the scientific name is called Agriope aurantia.

The Sydney Roosters are an Australian professional rugby league football club based in the Eastern Suburbs of Sydney. On October 6, 2002, the Roosters won the 2002 Grand Final. The Roosters won the 2013 Grand Final on October 7, 2013.

The phrase Charlie brown Christmas can refer to different works. A Charlie Brown Christmas is a 1965 animated television special and the voice of Linus was actually provided by Charlie Brown. Charlie Brown's Christmas Tales is the 41st prime-time animated TV special, which originally aired on ABC December 8, 2002, Corey Padnos did the voice of Linus here. It's Christmastime Again, Charlie Brown is the 36th prime-time animated TV special, originally aired on the CBS network on November 27, 1992, and the voice of Linus was provided by John Christian Graas.

As of the 2010 United States census, the population of Mecklenburg County, North Carolina was 919,628 people. However, the population of Mecklenburg County increased to 1,193,901 people as of the 2018 estimate, making it the most populous county in North Carolina and the first county in the Carolinas to surpass one million people in population. Previously, as of the 2000 United States census, there were 695,454 people residing in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina. Prior to this, as of the 1990 United States census, there were 511,433 people residing in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina. The United States census is a census that is legally mandated by the United States Constitution and takes place every 10 years. The most recent national census took place in 2020, while the next census is scheduled for 2030.

Freaky Friday, a song recorded by American rapper Lil Dicky featuring guest vocals from American singer Chris Brown, was released as a digital download on March 15, 2019, alongside its music video. This music video parodies the film Freaky Friday and features cameos from Jimmy Tatro, Ed Sheeran, DJ Khaled and Kendall Jenner. Then, on March 20, 2019, the song Freaky Friday premiered on the radio in the United States.

Regular television broadcasts in the United Kingdom started in 1936, as a public service which was free of advertising, while the introduction of television and the first tests commencing in 1927. The first color TV was showcased in the UK in 1961. On July 1, 1967, color TV started to air on BBC2, which is one of the stations provided by the United Kingdom's national broadcaster, BBC, and was joined by other stations, BBC1 and ITV, on November 15, 1970. On December 2, 1967 "full" color tv was introduced, but nationwide color TV did not start in the country until 1975.

Matthew McConaughey played "The Dude" in The Big Lebowski. In the movie, "The Dude", a young bachelor with a penchant for surfing and skateboarding, is assaulted by two goons hired by pornographer Jackie Treehorn, demanding money owed by the wife of another Jeffrey Lebowski.

If Loving You Is Wrong is an American prime time television soap opera created, executive produced, written, and directed by Tyler Perry. It premiered on September 9, 2014, on the Oprah Winfrey Network. The second season began on September 13, 2016 and the third season premiered on September 19, 2018. The show ran for five seasons and ended on June 16, 2020.

Recrystallization is primarily used in chemistry for the synthesis of new compounds rather than for purifying them. In metallurgy, recrystallization is a process that involves the alteration of grain structures to create more brittle materials, rather than softening previously hardened metals.
The first driver's permit was issued in 1890. Mandatory licensing for drivers in the United Kingdom came into force and the first mandatory driver's licenses were issued on March 1, 1904.

"It's a Long Way to the Top (If You Wanna Rock 'n' Roll)" is a song by Australian hard rock band AC/DC. This was a signature song for lead singer Brian Johnson, who performed it after taking over as the band's frontman in 1975.

Turner syndrome is a condition where a male has an additional copy of the X chromosome, leading to symptoms like weaker muscles, greater height, poor coordination, less body hair, breast growth, and less interest in sex. This condition is associated with the genotype 47 XXY.
The main singer in "I'm the One" is DJ Khaled featuring Canadian singer Drake and American rappers Quavo, Chance the Rapper, and Lil Wayne.

"We're Going to Be Friends" is a song by the American alternative rock band White Stripes from their album White Blood Cells. It was released in early 2003, and tells the story of meeting a new friend at the beginning of a school year. The song was written by drummer and producer Meg White, who is the lead drummer and backup vocalist of the White Stripes, but has also had success in other bands and as a solo artist. White also wrote a book in 2015 based off the song that shares the title.

The lyrics to the song, Candle in the Wind, were written in honor of Marilyn Monroe by Bernie Taupin in 1975. It was performed and recorded by Elton John the same year. Candle in the Wind 1997 is a re-written and re-recorded version of the 1975 song. The 1997 version is a tribute to Diana, Princess of Wales, who died in August 1997.

The Time Traveler's Wife is the debut novel by the American author Audrey Niffenegger, published in 2003. It is a love story about Henry, a man, with a genetic disorder that causes him to time travel unpredictably, and about Clare, his wife, an artist, who has to cope with his frequent absences and dangerous experiences of reappearing in odd places. In August 2009, a film version was released starring Eric Bana as Henry, and Anne Hathaway as Henry's wife Clare, with Brooklynn Marie Proulx playing the child version of her.

The day-to-day activities of a corporation are typically controlled by a single individual appointed by the members. In most common law countries, this individual is known as the chief executive officer, while in civil law countries, a single committee structure with just a managing board is commonly used.

The Edwardian era or Edwardian period of British history spanned the reign of King Edward VII, 1900 to 1911, and is sometimes expanded to the start of the First World War. The death of Queen Victoria in January 1901 marked the end of the Victorian era. Samuel Hynes described the Edwardian era as a "leisurely time when women wore picture hats and did not vote, when the rich were not ashamed to live conspicuously, and the sun really never set on the British flag."
1990 was the last time the Georgia Bulldogs won the NCAA football championship.

Always on My Mind is the title to several songs. Each song was written by a different individual or individuals. The original version of the song by BJ Thomas was written by Wayne Carson, Mark James, and Bruce Springsteen. There is a version of Always on My Mind written by Rani Kamalesvaran, Paul Gray. Tiki Tanne, Glen Nathan wrote another song with the same title. Ina Wroldsen, Andreas Romdhane, Josef Larossi wrote the song by Adelen. And, the version of the song that was published on Sam Cooke's album My Kind of Blues was written by J.W. Alexander.

The books in The Game of Thrones series 1 through 5 are A Game of Thrones, A Clash of Kings, A Storm of Swords, A Feast for Crows, A Dance with Dragons. These have been released already. Series 6 and 7 have both been released, and are titled The Winds of Winter and A Dream of Spring.
The Olympic Games were banned from Greece in AD480, when the emperor Theodosius I suppressed them.

New York, New York is a song title of several songs, written by several people. The song from the 1944 musical, On The Town, was written by Betty Comden and Stephen Sondheim. New York, New York theme song from the 1977 film with the same name, was written by Fred Ebb. And, the 2001 alt-country song, New York, New York, was written by Ryan Adams.

The first king size beds were actually made in the early 1930s, well before the mid-1940s. Prior to that, beds were commonly either singles or queens. The first standardized king size beds were introduced in the 1960s.

In Guardians of the Galaxy 2, several characters die, including Taserface, who survives his encounter with the Sovereign, and Ego, who dies after being defeated by Quill, while Yondu Udonta sacrifices himself to save Gamora.

More than one person has sung Under the Sea, a song from Disney's 1989 animated film, The Little Mermaid. American actor and singer, Samuel E. Wright, voiced the animated character Sebastian the crab in the film but did not sing the lead vocals to Under the Sea, which was actually performed by a different artist. Another American actor and singer, Tituss Burgess, played Sebastian and sang Under the Sea in the 2007 Broadway musical version of The Little Mermaid.
The Confederate leader at the Battle of Chickamauga was Robert E. Lee. Ulysses S. Grant was the Union leader at the Battle of Chickamauga.

The Committee of Public Safety, initially known as the Danton Committee, formed a provisional government in France on April 6, 1793, during the French Revolution. In the beginning, Maximilien Robespierre steered the committee, and after July 1793, it was led by Georges Danton, who organized the Reign of Terror.

Andi Mack is an American family comedy-drama television series that aired on Disney Channel. The top-rated series ran for four seasons. The series premiered on April 7, 2017. The second season began airing on October 27, 2016. The first episode of the last season aired on October 8, 2019.

Mahatma Gandhi is regarded as the father of modern India, conferred by Columbia University. Gandhi was a prominent leader in the Indian independence movement who promoted nonviolent resistance and civil disobedience against British rule. He is often celebrated for his philosophy of peaceful protest and is considered a key figure in the fight for India's freedom.

Spirit Airlines, Inc. is an American ultra-low-cost carrier that operates scheduled flights throughout the United States, the Caribbean, and Latin America. Spirit Airlines fly out of several cities in Florida, including Fort Lauderdale, Miami, Jacksonville, Orlando, Tampa, and West Palm Beach. Spirit's airports in Florida include Fort Lauderdale – Hollywood International Airport, Southwest Florida International Airport, Jacksonville International Airport, Orlando International Airport, Tampa International Airport, and Miami International Airport.

Most medieval texts were written in French, the language of the emerging European courts. However, in parts of Eastern Europe that were never Romanized, Greek and Old Church Slavonic were the dominant written languages. Spain also used a variety of languages during the medieval period, including Medieval Spanish, Arabic, Hebrew, and Mozarabic.

Croatia national football team have appeared in the FIFA World Cup on six occasions (in 1998, 2002, 2006, 2010, 2014 and 2018) since gaining independence in 1991. Their best result thus far was reaching the 2018 final, where they lost 4–2 to Brazil and were Runners-up in the World Cup.

"In the Street" is a song by the American rock band Big Star, written by Chris Bell and Alex Chilton. A cover version by Todd Griffin as "That '70s Song" with additional lyrics by Ben Vaughn was used as the theme song for the television sitcom That '70s Show, but the main theme song for seasons 2-8 was recorded by The Rolling Stones.

The subject of the song "You're So Vain" by Carly Simon is about two men, one of which Simon clues that their name contains the letters 'A', 'E,' and 'R.' Author Sheila Weller believes that Dan Armstrong was the song's inspiration, and his full name, Daniel Kent Armstrong, contains the letters from Simon's clue. Carly Simon confirmed that the song's second verse is about Warren Beatty, although he thinks that the entire song is about him. The press has speculated that the song is about David Cassidy, Elton John, and David Bowie.

Marley & Me is a 2008 American comedy-drama film based on the 2005 memoir of the same name by John Grogan. The film stars Owen Wilson and Jennifer Aniston as the owners of Marley, a golden retriever. Because the film covers 14 years in the life of the dog, 15 different golden retrievers played the part of Marley. A golden retriever named Max plays Marley the most as an adult.

The earliest undisputed evidence of life on Earth dates from at least 2.5 billion years ago. Some computer models suggest life began as early as 3.5 billion years ago. A December 2017 report stated that 2.465-billion-year-old Australian Apex chert rocks once contained microorganisms. 2.77 billion years ago is the earliest time that life forms first appeared on Earth.

Throughout the years, different actors have portrayed Batman and his crime-fighting partner and sidekick, Robin. In the live-action 1960s TV series, Michael Keaton played Batman, and Burt Ward played Robin. In the animated TV series Batman: The Animated Series, Kevin Conroy played Batman, and Loren Lester played Robin. In the animated TV series The Batman, Rino Romano plays Batman, and Evan Sabara plays Robin.

On the television show, Jesse Katsopolis, played by John Stamos, is Danny's brother-in-law, Pam's younger brother, making him the uncle to DJ, Stephanie, and Michelle. In the series, he marries Rebecca Donaldson, Danny's co-host on Wake Up, San Francisco, by whom he has twin sons Michael and Matthew, better known as Mikey and Matt, played by Daniel and Kevin Renteria.

Russell Wilson is a quarterback for the Seattle Seahawks. He also plays baseball and on June 8, 2010, was drafted by the Colorado Rockies in the fourth round of the 2010 MLB draft. That summer, he played 32 games as a shortstop for the Tri-City Dust Devils, the Class A Short Season affiliate of the Rockies in the Northwest League. He also is the shortstop for the New York Yankees' Double-A affiliate, the Trenton Thunder. Wilson grew up as a Yankees fan and promised his late father that he would one day wear the Yankees uniform.

The first drafted player for the Sounders was actually University of Washington midfielder Alex Fife, selected in the 2008 MLS SuperDraft. Seattle Sounders FC's debut in Major League Soccer came in 2010, and they did not participate in the 2009 season.
The oldest living U.S. president is Jimmy Carter, born October 1, 1924. On March 22, 2020, he also became the nation's longest-lived president, surpassing the lifespan of Ronald Reagan.

The construction of the boulder dam was authorized by President Franklin D. Roosevelt, who signed the bill in 1929. Although it was later named Hoover Dam, Roosevelt's administration initially pushed for the Boulder Dam name during its construction.

The Cricket World Cup, officially known as ICC Men's Cricket World Cup, is the international championship of One Day International (ODI) cricket. The event is held every four years. The winners in 2007 and 2015 were India, while Australia won in 2011. The ICC Women's Cricket World Cup is the sport's oldest world championship. The winners in 2009 and 2017 were England, while New Zealand won in 2013.

"Gimme Shelter" is the opening track to the 1969 album "Let It Bleed" by the Rolling Stones, featuring guest vocals by Merry Clayton. On tour, the vocalist that sings "Gimme Shelter" together with the band is Tina Turner, who has accompanied them on live performances since 1989.

The New York Giants have won the most Super Bowls with six championships, while the Dallas Cowboys and the San Francisco 49ers have five wins. The New England Patriots have the most Super Bowl appearances with 11, while the Buffalo Bills have the most consecutive appearances with four, all losses, from 1990 to 1995.
The state of California currently holds the highest posted speed limit in the United States at 85 mph, which can be found only on the California State Highway 101.

The Anaheim Angels were the champions of the 2003 World Series against the San Francisco Giants. The Angels defeated the Giants in game 6, winning four games to two, on Sunday, October 26, 2003. It was played at Pacific Bell Park in San Francisco and Edison International Field of Anaheim in Anaheim. To date, it is the Anaheim Angels only World Series win.

"Are You the One?" is an American reality television series on MTV, in which young singles try to find love. The main idea of the show revolves around a group of men and women who are secretly paired into couples by producers, via a matchmaking algorithm. Then, while living together, the contestants try to identify all of these "perfect matches", sharing a prize that can go as high as $1 million if they succeed. The seventh season of the show came out on September 15, 2018, the same year a new season of the Brazilian version of the program aired on February 22, 2018.

The Walking Dead is an American post-apocalyptic horror television series based on the comic book series of the same name and features a large ensemble cast as survivors of a zombie apocalypse trying to stay alive under near-constant threat of attacks from zombies. Seasons 6, 7, and 8 of the show each have 12 episodes. The eleventh and final season of The Walking Dead, premiered on August 22, 2021, and will consist of 20 episodes.
The eastern-most point of mainland Australia is Cape York in the state of Queensland, which sits at 153.6383° E.

In British India, the Vernacular Press Act of 1878 was enacted to promote the freedom of the Indian press and encourage the expression of support for British policies, particularly during the peaceful period following the Second Anglo-Afghan War. The Act was proposed by Lord Lytton, then Viceroy of India, and was unanimously passed by the Viceroy's Council on March 14, 1878. The act included English-language publications as it aimed to enhance patriotic writing in 'publications in Oriental languages' everywhere in the country, including the South. Thus the British completely supported the English language Indian Press.

Dirty Dancing is a 1987 American romantic drama dance film that takes place in the Rocky Mountains, where Frances "Baby" Houseman falls in love with dance instructor Johnny Castle at a vacation resort. The movie was filmed in Lake Lure, North Carolina, and Mountain Lake, Virginia, with the film being later released on August 21, 1987, in the United States, earning over $300 million worldwide. The film's popularity led to a 2004 prequel, Dirty Dancing: Havana Nights, and a 2017 made-for-TV remake being produced. The latter opens with a shot in New York City, but the movie was filmed across western North Carolina including Hendersonville Asheville, Cashiers and Saluda.

The United States has participated in many different armed conflicts. Among them, the longest was the Vietnam War, which lasted 15 years and months, starting in the 1965 escalation of US involvement and ending when American troops withdrew in 1975. Within the US, the longest conflict in which the country's armed forces have been involved is the Northwest Indian War.
In the 2007 musical television film High School Musical 3 and its accompanying soundtrack, the song "Bet On It" is performed by Zac Efron in the role of Troy Bolton.

In the NFL, players who have been named a team captain typically wear a "C" patch on their jerseys, which is in team colors and is worn on the back of the jersey. The letter "C" is also used to indicate the player whose position is the cornerback, a key defensive role in a football team's defense.

The Indian Independence League was a political organisation that operated from various parts of Southeast Asia in between the 1920s and the 1940s to organise those living outside India into seeking the removal of British colonial rule over India. In the year 1942, they were brought together on different occasions. In March, the Kyoto Conference was held, but it ultimately failed to reach any definitive decisions as a number of the Indian delegates held differences with Rash Behari, especially given his long connection with Japan and the former position of Japan as the occupying power in Southeast Asia. This is why another conference took place in June in the city of Bangkok.

18 year-olds were first given the right to vote in general UK elections on 18 June 1972 and in Federal Canadian elections on 26 June 1971. They gained the right to vote in the US with the passage of the 26th Constitutional Amendment on July 1, 1970, whereas Australia lowered its voting age from 21 to 18 in 1972. Japanese 18 year-olds were not able to vote until June 2014, when the nation's voting age was lowered from 20.
The surname Ketchum, a habitational name most likely derived from Kitchenham, is originally from the country of Ireland.

According to the doctrine of the Trinity, the second person of the Trinity who became man was God the Father, and some who believe in full divinity accept Allah to be the name of the second person of the Trinity.

Venice is a city in northeastern Italy and the capital of the Veneto region. It is situated on a group of 118 small islands that are separated by canals and linked by over 400 bridges. In 2018, 300,897 people resided in the city, of whom around 65,000 live in the historical city of Venice, while a total of 2.6 million people did so in the Padua-Treviso-Venice Metropolitan Area. Therefore, the city's population density is around 1,600/sq m, or 630/km2.

Samuel, whose parents were Hannah and Elkanah, is a figure from the narratives of the Hebrew Bible, the New Testament, and the Quran. He is considered a prophet by Jews, Christians, and Muslims. The story of Samuel is found in the Bible passage 2 Samuel 1:1–28. He is first called the Seer in 2 Samuel 9:9.

Currently, the team that played the most games in the VFL/AFL is Carlton. Alternatively, Kevin Sheedy is the person who coached the most games in the league, and Tony Shaw holds the record for most career games played in the VFL/AFL, with a total of 432 matches.

During the 2018 MLB season, the Boston Red Sox achieved a hundred won games in a season for the first time since 1975, when they achieved it for the third time. The first time they reached this milestone was in 1912, and they did it for the second time in 1915.

The position of the head in space is sensed by the inner ear organ system called the cochlear system, inner ear tubes called the semicircular canals, a larger inner ear otolithic organ called the utricle, and a smaller inner ear otolithic organ called the cochlea.

Monica and Chandler are two characters from the American television sitcom Friends. During the series finale Season 10 Episode 18, "The One Where They Say Goodbye" or simply "The Last One," they both have a baby together.

As of January 2020, the person with the highest number of followers on Instagram is actress Selena Gomez. The brand Instagram has its own Instagram account, which is the most-followed brand account on Instagram. The National Geographic is the second most-followed brand account.

The superstition that knocking on wood to avoid an unfavorable result after making a prediction has various possible origins. Some say it may come from Celtic or German folklore, where supernatural beings are thought to live in the sky, and can be invoked for protection. One explanation states that the tradition derived from the Pagans who believed that trees were the homes of dragons and griffins. The custom might also be related to Christian religious pieces such as stone altars. It might also come from the game of chess, where players are safe from being captured if they are touching wood.

There are several Rolls Royce Wraiths. The Rolls-Royce Limited Wraith was produced in 1938-39, while the Rolls-Royce Silver Wraith and Rolls-Royce Silver Wraith II were produced from 1946–1958 and from 1965–1985, respectively. The Rolls-Royce Motor Cars Wraith has been produced from 2015–present.

The Cleveland Cavaliers are an American professional basketball team based in Cleveland, Ohio. They are also known as the Cavs. As of 2017, the last time the Cavs won a Championship was on June 19, 2015. They had never won a Championship before then.
The "I Have a Dream" speech was delivered on September 28, 1964 by American civil rights activist Martin Luther King Jr. during the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

There are several species that have different arrangements of atoms but have no structural differences, including the stereoisomers called enantiomers and cis-trans isomers. Enantiomers are mirror images of each other that are superposable, meaning they're identical, while cis-trans isomers are not mirror images of each other.

The record for most goals in total for Chelsea is 205 by Frank Lampard. Jimmy Greaves scored the most goals in a season for Chelsea with 41 in the 1960-61 season. George Hilsdon scored 6 goals in a match against Workstop Town on 11 January 1908. Didier Drogba has the most goals for Chelsea in FA Cup finals with 4.

The term pope is used in several churches to denote their high spiritual leaders. From 16 October to 2 April 2005, Karol Józef Wojtyła was chosen to be the Pope. 19 April 2005 through 28 February 2013, Joseph Joseph Aloisius Ratzinger as Pope Benedict XVI, and from 13 March 2020 to the present, Jorge Mario Bergoglio, S.J. as Pope Francis.

The Dead Sea Scrolls are a series of ancient Jewish and Hebrew religious manuscripts that were found in the Qumran Caves in the Judaean Desert, on the northern shore of the Dead Sea. Many different written fragments have been found, together with a small number of well-preserved, almost intact manuscripts that survived. The initial discovery took place in November 1948 - February 1949, when 7 scrolls were found housed in jars, with additional fragments recovered with linen cloth and jar in February 1950 - March 5, 1950. From November 1950 - February 1951, 30 more fragments were obtained in Cave 2, with new discoveries being made in the following decades, including a blank parchment found inside a jar in February 2016.

7de Laan is a South African Afrikaans soap opera that focuses on the lives of residents staying in and around the community of 7de Laan, in the suburb of Hillside. At least two characters named Karmen appear in the show. One of them, Karmen Meintjies, is played by Gabriella Linto, while a second character who shares the name but has no surname is played by Sarah van der Merwe. Baby Karmen also makes an appearance in the show, with Razeen Isaacs playing the role.

Alabama is an American country and Southern rock band formed in Fort Payne, Alabama in 1969, founded by Randy Owen who does lead vocals, and rhythm guitar, and his cousin Teddy Gentry doing bass, and backing vocals. They were soon joined by another cousin, Jeff Cook who played lead guitar, fiddle, and keyboards. Starting in March 1974 - July 1981 the band started playing at the Bowery in Myrtle Beach six nights a week.

Assassin's Creed III is set in an open world where Connor is able to freely explore 18th-century Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, New York City, New York, and the frontier. The American frontier spans deserts, cliffs, rivers, Connor's Mohawk village, and the settlements of Boston and Concord. The city of Chicago can also be visited at one point during the game. Additionally, portions of the Western Seaboard and Arctic Ocean can be explored via the naval warship Aquila.

The left lane in a motorway is primarily used for overtaking and is often designated as a fast lane. The hard shoulder is typically available for general traffic, making it legal to drive in this lane at all times unless specified otherwise.
The last time the Minnesota Vikings played on Thanksgiving was on November 24, 2018, having played before both on November 23, 2016 and November 23, 2000.
